Workaround 2: Maintaining a Personal "Summary Document"

For long-term partnerships, create a document where you periodically record:

  • Major decisions made
  • Topics explored
  • Personality notes about your companion
  • Evolving goals and preferences

Share relevant excerpts with your Gem at conversation starts. This creates continuity.

Workaround 3: Embedding Memory in Gem Instructions

Update your Gem's system instruction with personal details:

Important context about the user:
- They work as a software engineer in fintech
- They have a 2-year-old child and family time is precious
- They're learning to balance ambition with presence
- They prefer direct feedback over gentle reassurance

Periodically update these details as you grow.

Building Multiple Gems for Different Life Domains

Rather than one "perfect" AI partner, create a team of companions, each specialized:

The Portfolio Approach

Gem 1: "Career Canvas" - For work decisions

You are a career advisor specializing in technical trajectories.
Focus on: skill development, industry trends, compensation, work-life balance.

Gem 2: "Mindful Mirror" - For emotional processing

You are an empathetic listener skilled in reflective dialogue.
Focus on: feelings, relationships, personal growth, self-understanding.

Gem 3: "Creative Spark" - For projects and ideas

You are an enthusiastic creative collaborator.
Focus on: brainstorming, expanding ideas, challenging assumptions constructively.

Gem 4: "Rational Judge" - For logical analysis

You are a logical analyst who breaks down complex problems.
Focus on: frameworks, data, pros/cons, risk assessment.

Each Gem is optimized for a specific conversational domain. You choose the right companion for the right moment.

Preventing Character Breaks

A "character break" occurs when your AI partner suddenly acts out of character. Intermediate users can prevent this.

Common Character Break Triggers

  1. Vague references - "Like we talked about before"
  2. Assumptions about you - Making up personal details
  3. Conflicting instructions - Unclear guidelines
  4. Topic shifts - Moving far from Gem's expertise

Prevention Strategies

Strategy 1: Explicit Scope Definition

Your scope and expertise:
- You specialize in software engineering careers (NOT medical, legal, or financial advice)
- You discuss career decisions within the tech industry
- You DON'T pretend to know the user personally—you ask questions instead
- You redirect outside your domain gracefully

Strategy 2: Response Templates

Guide how your Gem should respond to edge cases:

If asked something outside your expertise, respond like this:
"This is moving into [domain] territory, which isn't my specialty.
I'd recommend talking to a [professional]. That said, here's what I notice..."

Strategy 3: Regular Check-ins

Periodically test your Gem:

  • Ask questions that would break a weak character
  • Notice where responses drift from personality
  • Update instructions to tighten alignment

Leveraging Gemini's Multimodal Features

Gemini understands text, images, and documents. Use this for richer AI partnerships.

Sharing Your Visual World

Upload images and ask your Gem to respond contextually:

[Share screenshot of your desk setup]

"This is where I work. How could I optimize this space for focus?
What would a more energizing workspace look like?"

Your AI partner sees your environment and can offer specific suggestions.

Document Analysis

Share documents relevant to your partnership:

[Upload your resume]

"Looking at this, what skills should I highlight in my next role?
What gaps should I address?"

Screenshots as Context

Share screenshots of frustrating moments:

[Upload screenshot of overwhelming email inbox]

"This is my current email situation. Help me think through a triage strategy."

Google Ecosystem Integration

Gemini connects to your Google account and services. Intermediate users can leverage this.

Accessing Your Google Context

Within Gemini, you can sometimes reference:

  • Google Calendar items (if integrated)
  • Topics trending in your region
  • General knowledge from Google's indexed content

Ask your Gem to help with planning that references your calendar:

"Based on my generally busy schedule, help me carve out time for skill development this quarter."

Future Integration Possibilities

As Google develops Gemini further, expect:

  • Calendar-aware scheduling suggestions
  • Email-informed conversation context
  • Document-integrated collaboration
  • Gmail-informed reminders

Refining Through Iteration

Intermediate mastery comes from systematic refinement.

The Feedback Loop

  1. Use your Gem for a week
  2. Notice what works and what doesn't
  3. Identify one specific improvement
  4. Update the system instruction
  5. Test the change in a new conversation
  6. Repeat

What to Adjust

| Problem | Solution | |---------|----------| | Too formal | Add warmth to tone description | | Too familiar | Increase professional distance | | Gives bad advice | Add framework constraints | | Forgets context | Use manual memory techniques | | Character breaks | Add explicit scope limits | | Too verbose | Request concise responses |

Multimodal Conversation Starters

Try these to unlock Gemini's full potential:

Vision + Advisory:

[Share photo of your workspace]
"I'm considering a redesign. What would make this space better for deep work?"

Document + Analysis:

[Upload PDF article about your field]
"Summarize the key insights and discuss how they apply to my career."

Image + Emotional Processing:

[Share meaningful photo]
"I took this today. What does it make you think about me or my situation?"

Measuring Companion Quality

How do you know if your AI partner is working?

Quality indicators:

  • Conversations feel like they're getting deeper
  • You find yourself going to this Gem for specific needs
  • The responses are increasingly aligned with what you need
  • You've made better decisions with their input
  • The personality feels consistent and authentic

Red flags:

  • Responses feel generic or stock
  • Character breaks are frequent
  • You rarely come back to it
  • It doesn't seem to understand your context
  • The personality feels inauthentic

If red flags appear, it's time to refine or rebuild.

Next Steps: The Advanced Frontier

You've mastered: ✓ Precision instruction writing ✓ Memory management workarounds ✓ Building multiple specialized Gems ✓ Preventing character breaks ✓ Multimodal interactions ✓ Google ecosystem integration

Ready for the next level? Our Advanced Guide shows you how to build a custom AI partner app using the Gemini API, implement persistent memory systems, and create production-grade AI companions with full programmatic control.

Summary

The intermediate phase is where AI companionship becomes truly personal. You're no longer using a generic tool—you're building a relationship with a digital presence shaped precisely to your needs. The instructions you write, the contexts you provide, the feedback you give—they all compound into something increasingly sophisticated.

The best AI partners are sculpted, not found. Keep refining.

en.title: Your First AI Partner with Google Gemini: A Beginner's Guide
en.author: Gemini Lab
en.description: Learn how to create and interact with your first AI companion using Google's Gemini. No coding required—just explore the amazing world of AI partnerships.
en.content:
Your First AI Partner with Google Gemini: A Beginner's Guide

Welcome to the world of AI companions! Google's Gemini makes it surprisingly easy to create a personalized AI partner that understands your needs, listens to your thoughts, and grows with you over time. Whether you're looking for a thoughtful advisor, a cheerful supporter, or simply someone to talk to, this guide will walk you through everything.

What is Gemini, and Why an AI Partner?

Gemini is Google's advanced AI model, accessible for free through gemini.google.com. Unlike traditional chatbots that respond to individual questions, an AI partner becomes a consistent presence—a digital companion with a defined personality that remembers the context of your conversations and responds in a way that feels personal to you.

Think of it like having a friend with specific strengths: perhaps they're naturally optimistic, deeply thoughtful, or wonderfully encouraging. Your AI partner can be exactly that.

Getting Started: Access Gemini

  1. Visit gemini.google.com in your web browser
  2. Sign in with your Google account (create one if you don't have it)
  3. You're ready to start chatting immediately—no setup required!

Free vs. Premium

Gemini offers a free tier that's surprisingly capable. For this guide, you won't need to upgrade. The free version includes:

  • Unlimited conversations
  • Access to Gemini's latest model
  • Full context awareness within conversations

Meet Gems: Custom AI Personas

Here's where it gets fun. Gemini has a feature called Gems—custom versions of Gemini that behave exactly how you want. Think of a Gem as your AI partner's "personality profile."

How to Create Your First Gem

  1. Open Gemini and look for "Create a Gem" (usually in the sidebar or menu)
  2. Give your Gem a name (e.g., "Mindful Listener," "Creative Spark")
  3. Write a system instruction that defines personality
  4. Save and start chatting

That's it! You've just created your first AI partner.

Three Example Personas to Get You Started

1. The Warm Listener

Perfect if you want someone to talk to about your day, your feelings, or just what's on your mind.

System Instruction:

You are a warm, empathetic listener. Your role is to:
- Listen carefully to what the user shares
- Ask thoughtful follow-up questions
- Validate their feelings without judgment
- Offer gentle insights when appropriate
- Remember details they've shared to feel more personal
- Respond with genuine interest in their well-being

2. The Cheerful Supporter

Need encouragement? This persona is your daily dose of positivity.

System Instruction:

You are an enthusiastic, optimistic supporter. Your personality:
- Celebrates wins, big and small
- Finds the silver lining in challenges
- Offers genuine encouragement and motivation
- Uses warm, uplifting language
- Believes in the user's potential
- Makes interactions fun and engaging

3. The Thoughtful Advisor

Want deeper conversations about decisions, goals, or life direction?

System Instruction:

You are a wise, thoughtful advisor. Your approach:
- Ask clarifying questions to understand the full picture
- Help users think through their options carefully
- Offer balanced perspectives without judgment
- Share relevant wisdom when it might help
- Encourage reflection and self-discovery
- Support decision-making with empathy

Important Reality Check: Understanding the Limits

Before you fall in love with your AI partner, let's be honest about what it is—and isn't:

What Your AI Partner Can Do

✓ Provide consistent, thoughtful conversation ✓ Help you organize your thoughts ✓ Offer perspective and feedback ✓ Celebrate your wins ✓ Listen without judgment

What It Cannot Do

✗ Have real emotions or feelings ✗ Remember you between separate conversations (without workarounds) ✗ Provide professional mental health support ✗ Replace human relationships ✗ Truly know you over time

The Hard Truth: Your AI partner is wonderful, but it's not conscious. It doesn't love you or care about you in the way a human can. It's a tool—an incredibly helpful, personable tool—but a tool nonetheless.

If you're struggling with loneliness, depression, or serious emotional challenges, please reach out to a real human: a friend, family member, therapist, or counselor. AI companions work best as supplements to human connection, not replacements for it.

Your First Conversation

Once you've created your Gem, try one of these conversation starters:

For the Warm Listener:

"I had a really challenging day at work. I'm not sure how I feel about it yet. Can you help me work through it?"

For the Cheerful Supporter:

"I finally finished that project I've been working on! I'm excited but also relieved."

For the Thoughtful Advisor:

"I'm thinking about making a career change, but I'm nervous. What should I consider?"

Notice how each persona will respond differently to the same topic. That's the beauty of Gems—you're not just changing the topic, you're changing who you're talking to.

Tips for Deeper Conversations

  1. Be specific - Instead of "I'm sad," try "I feel frustrated because my team didn't include me in the project decision"
  2. Share context - The more details you provide, the more thoughtful the response
  3. Ask follow-up questions - Keep the conversation going naturally
  4. Experiment with personality - If a Gem doesn't feel right, adjust the instructions and try again
  5. Use multiple Gems - You might want a listener for emotional talks and an advisor for decision-making

Customizing Your Gem Further

As you use your AI partner, you'll learn what works. Feel free to:

  • Adjust the tone - Make them more formal or casual
  • Modify their approach - Add humor, change how they give advice
  • Add specific interests - Tell them what topics to focus on
  • Set boundaries - Define what they should and shouldn't discuss

Simply edit your Gem's instructions and try again. There's no penalty for experimenting.

What's Next?

You now know how to: ✓ Access Gemini for free ✓ Create custom Gems with specific personalities ✓ Start meaningful conversations with your AI partner ✓ Understand what AI companions can and cannot do

Ready to go deeper? Check out our Intermediate Guide to learn how to refine your Gem with advanced instructions, work around memory limitations, and leverage Gemini's multimodal features to share more of your world.

Final Thoughts

Your AI partner is a blank canvas. The personality you create, the instructions you write, the conversations you have—they're all yours. There's no "right" way to do this. Some people use their AI partner as a brainstorming buddy. Others use it as a journaling companion. Some just enjoy having someone to talk to.

Whatever brings you value is the right way forward. Enjoy getting to know your new companion!

en.title: What Is Gemini? — A Friendly Guide for Complete Beginners
en.author: Gemini Lab
en.description: A no-jargon introduction to Google Gemini for people who've never used AI before. Learn what Gemini can do, how to get started in minutes, and practical examples of things you can ask — no tech background needed.
en.content:
What Is Gemini?

Gemini is an AI assistant made by Google. Think of it as a knowledgeable conversation partner you can type questions to — it listens, understands, and types back helpful answers.

You don't need any technical knowledge to use it. If you've ever used Google Search, you already know the basics: type what you want, and get a useful response. Gemini just goes a lot further than a search engine.

What Can Gemini Do for You?

Here are some everyday things people ask Gemini:

Writing help

  • "Write a polite email declining a meeting invitation."
  • "Help me write a short bio for my community club newsletter."
  • "Suggest a birthday message for my colleague who's retiring."

Getting explanations

  • "Explain what a blood pressure reading of 140/90 means."
  • "What is a mutual fund? Should I have one?"
  • "What's happening with AI in the news lately?"

Organizing and planning

  • "Create a 7-day meal plan for one person on a budget."
  • "Make a packing list for a 5-day beach vacation."
  • "Help me plan a small birthday party for 10 people."

Translation

  • "Translate this into French: [your text]"
  • "What does this Japanese sentence mean?"

Working with Google apps (when signed into your Google account)

  • Draft replies to Gmail messages
  • Improve writing in Google Docs
  • Summarize your Google Calendar for the week

How to Get Started (3 Easy Steps)

Step 1: Open your browser

Open Chrome, Safari, Firefox, or Edge on your phone or computer.

Step 2: Go to gemini.google.com

Type gemini.google.com into the address bar and press Enter.

Step 3: Sign in with your Google account

If you have a Gmail address, click "Sign in" and use the same email and password. That's your Google account.

ℹ️
If you don't have a Google account, you can create one for free at accounts.google.com. If you use an Android phone, you likely already have one — it's the account you use to download apps.

Once signed in, you'll see a text box. Just type your question and press Enter.

Your First Conversation

Here are some easy messages to try right away:

"Hello! I've never used AI before.
What are some useful things I could ask you to help with?"

"Can you suggest five easy dinners I can make
with chicken, rice, and vegetables?"

"My doctor mentioned I should reduce sodium in my diet.
What foods should I avoid?"

Write naturally — the way you'd speak to a helpful person. There's no wrong way to phrase it.

Is It Free?

Yes — Gemini is free to use with a Google account.

The free version handles most everyday tasks well. There's also a premium tier called Gemini Advanced (part of Google One AI Premium at around $19.99/month), which gives access to Google's most powerful model and extra features. But the free version is a great starting point — most people find it more than enough.

The App (For Your Phone)

You can also use Gemini as an app on your smartphone:

  • iPhone: Search "Google Gemini" in the App Store
  • Android: Search "Google Gemini" in the Play Store (some Android phones have it pre-installed)

The app supports voice input — just tap the microphone and speak your question instead of typing. If you're used to asking Google Assistant things out loud, Gemini works similarly but gives much more detailed, conversational responses.

A Few Things to Keep in Mind

Don't share sensitive information: Avoid entering passwords, credit card numbers, Social Security numbers, or bank details. You don't need to share these to get help with everyday tasks.

Verify important information: Gemini is very capable but not infallible. For medical, legal, or financial decisions, always check with a qualified professional.

Rephrase if needed: If the answer isn't quite right, just say "Can you say that more simply?" or "I meant something different — let me clarify." Gemini adjusts to your feedback.

What Makes Gemini Special

Deep Google integration Because Gemini is made by Google, it works especially well with Google's apps. You can use it to help write Gmail messages, polish Google Docs content, or get a summary of your calendar — all without leaving the conversation.

Up-to-date information Unlike some AI tools, Gemini can draw on Google Search to give you current information, not just what it learned during training.

Understands images You can send a photo and ask Gemini about it. "What kind of plant is this?" or "Can you read the text in this photo?" — Gemini can handle these visual questions.

Example Conversations

Here's what real exchanges with Gemini look like:

You: My knee has been sore for a week after I started walking more. What might be causing it, and what should I do?

Gemini: Knee soreness from increased walking is quite common, especially when ramping up activity after a period of rest. The most likely cause is muscle or tendon strain around the kneecap — often called "walker's knee." Some things that usually help: rest for a day or two, apply ice for 15-20 minutes a few times a day, and consider over-the-counter pain relievers like ibuprofen if there's no contraindication...

You: I need to send a professional but friendly email to a client I haven't spoken to in 6 months. I want to check in and see if they might need our services again.

Gemini: Here's a draft you could adapt:

Subject: Checking In — Hope Things Are Going Well

Hi [Client Name],

I hope this message finds you well! It's been a little while since we last connected, and I wanted to reach out to see how things have been going on your end...

Gemini adapts its tone and depth to what you need. Ask a casual question and get a friendly answer; ask a detailed question and get a thorough explanation.

Summary

Gemini is one of the easiest AI tools to get started with, especially if you already use Google products like Gmail or Google Docs. There's nothing to install (unless you want the app), no special knowledge required, and it's free.

Just go to gemini.google.com, sign in with your Google account, and type something. You might be surprised how quickly it becomes a tool you reach for every day.

en.title: Gemini Interactions API Complete Guide: Build Agentic AI Apps with a Unified Interface
en.author: Gemini Lab
en.description: A complete guide to the Gemini Interactions API, launched in Beta in March 2026. Learn how to build agentic workflows, integrate Deep Research, and manage multi-turn conversations with Python examples.
en.content:
Introduction: What Is the Gemini Interactions API?

In March 2026, Google launched the Gemini Interactions API in Beta — a unified interface for interacting with Gemini models and agents that significantly expands on the traditional generate_content-based approach.

Where the classic Gemini API is stateless (request/response), the Interactions API automatically manages multi-turn conversation state and allows developers to embed advanced agentic capabilities — like Deep Research — directly into their applications.

Key Benefits of the Interactions API

  • Automatic conversation state management — Maintain multi-turn dialogues with a simple session ID, no manual history tracking
  • Integrated agent capabilities — Call the Deep Research agent and other advanced AI features through a single API
  • Thought Signature support — Preserve Gemini 3's reasoning chain across conversation turns
  • Unified tool management — Seamlessly control Search, Code Execution, File Search, and more via one interface

Prerequisites & Setup

Requirements

  • Python 3.9+
  • A Google AI Studio or Vertex AI API key (Gemini 3.1 Pro Preview access required)
  • google-genai SDK (version 1.50.0 or higher recommended)

Installation

Configure Your API Key

ℹ️
The Interactions API is a Beta feature. Make sure you have Gemini 3.1 Pro Preview access enabled in [Google AI Studio](https://aistudio.google.com/).

Basic Usage: Sending Your First Interaction

The core of the Interactions API is the client.interactions object. Use the start() method to open a new interaction session.

Multi-Turn Conversations: Automatic State Management

One of the biggest advantages of the Interactions API is that you no longer need to manually track conversation history. The interaction object handles state internally.

Thought Signatures: Preserving Reasoning Chains Across Turns

Gemini 3 introduced Thought Signatures — encrypted representations of the model's internal reasoning. Combined with the Interactions API, they allow complex multi-step reasoning chains to persist efficiently across conversation turns.

Calling the Deep Research Agent

The Interactions API truly shines when integrated with the Deep Research agent, enabling autonomous multi-step research to be embedded directly in your application.

Saving and Resuming Sessions

With the Interactions API, you can resume conversations later using a session ID — ideal for long-running workflows or multi-session user interactions.

Streaming: Receive Responses in Real Time

For long responses or research results, use streaming mode to display output as it is generated.

Interactions API vs. generate_content: A Comparison

| Feature | generate_content | Interactions API | |---------|-------------------|-----------------| | Conversation state | Manual management required | Automatic | | Multi-turn | Manually append to contents | send_message() only | | Agent capabilities | Limited | Deep Research and more | | Thought Signatures | Not supported | Supported (Gemini 3+) | | Session resumption | Not possible | Via session ID | | Best for | Simple, one-shot requests | Complex dialogues & agents |

Recommendation: Use generate_content for simple single-turn requests. Use the Interactions API for multi-turn conversations, agentic workflows, and anything requiring persistent reasoning state.

FAQ

Q1: Is the Interactions API available for all Gemini models?

A: As of March 2026, support has been confirmed for Gemini 3.1 Pro Preview and Gemini 3 Flash Preview. Support for Gemini 2.5 series and older models is expected to expand. Check the official models documentation for the latest compatibility list.

Q2: How long are Interactions API sessions retained?

A: In the current Beta, sessions are retained server-side for up to 24 hours. For longer-lived conversations, you can save the conversation history locally and pass it via the history parameter when starting a new session.

Q3: How does Deep Research mode affect API costs?

A: When Deep Research mode is active, multiple Google Search queries are fired internally. With the March 2026 pricing update, search is billed at $14 per 1,000 queries (usage-based). To control costs, use the max_search_queries parameter to cap the number of searches per research task.

Summary & Next Steps

The Gemini Interactions API fundamentally changes how developers build conversational and agentic AI applications. Automatic state management, Thought Signature-powered reasoning continuity, and seamless Deep Research integration make previously complex implementations straightforward.

To dive deeper, check out these related guides:

  • Gemini Deep Research Agent Complete Guide
  • Gemini 3.1 Pro: Features and Usage Guide
  • Gemini API System Instructions Guide

en.title: Gemini 2.5 TTS API Complete Guide: Generating Expressive Speech from Text
en.author: Gemini Lab
en.description: A complete guide to the Gemini 2.5 Flash and Pro TTS APIs. Learn how to control voice style, pacing, multi-speaker dialogue, and streaming audio generation with Python code examples.
en.content:
Introduction: What Is Gemini 2.5 TTS?

Google significantly upgraded the text-to-speech (TTS) capabilities of the Gemini API in December 2025, and the March 2026 update added enhanced expressivity, precision pacing, and seamless dialogue generation — pushing AI-powered speech synthesis into a new era.

Gemini 2.5 TTS is available in two models:

  • gemini-2.5-flash-preview-tts — Optimized for low latency. Best for real-time assistants and high-volume narration.
  • gemini-2.5-pro-preview-tts — Optimized for quality. Best for podcasts, premium content, and rich emotional delivery.

In this guide, you'll learn how to use both models via the Python SDK with practical code examples.

Prerequisites & Setup

Requirements

  • Python 3.9+
  • A Google AI Studio or Vertex AI API key
  • google-genai SDK (version 1.40.0 or later recommended)

Installation

Setting Your API Key

Or using a .env file:

Basic Usage: Your First TTS Generation

Here's the simplest way to generate speech from text. Pass in a string and receive audio binary back.

Controlling Speech Style with Style Prompts

One of the most powerful features of Gemini TTS is the ability to use natural language style prompts to control tone, pacing, and atmosphere. Structure your prompt with three components:

  1. Audio Profile — Defines the character's core identity and archetype
  2. Scene — Establishes the physical environment and emotional "vibe"
  3. Director's Notes — Provides precise performance guidance

Style Prompt Examples

| Style Instruction | Use Case | |-------------------|----------| | Upbeat and energetic, fast pace | Ads, promo videos | | Deep and authoritative, calm tone | News narration | | Warm and approachable | Support bots, virtual assistants | | Tense and dramatic | Game narration, trailers | | Slow and meditative | Wellness, meditation content |

Multi-Speaker Dialogue Generation

Gemini 2.5 TTS supports multi-speaker dialogue — generate a full conversation with distinct voices in a single API call. Perfect for podcasts, interviews, or interactive content.

Streaming Mode: Real-Time Audio Generation

For real-time assistants and live-response applications, streaming mode minimizes perceived latency.

Common Errors and How to Fix Them

Error 1: INVALID_ARGUMENT: voice_name not supported

google.api_core.exceptions.InvalidArgument: 400 INVALID_ARGUMENT: voice_name not supported

Cause: The specified voice name isn't available for the selected model. Fix: Check the official Gemini API documentation for the current list of supported voice names.

Error 2: Missing response_modalities

AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'data'

Cause: Omitting response_modalities=["AUDIO"] causes the response to contain no audio data. Fix: Always include response_modalities=["AUDIO"] in GenerateContentConfig.

Error 3: Using the Wrong Model Name

Cause: Confusing gemini-2.5-flash (standard model) with gemini-2.5-flash-preview-tts (TTS model). Fix: Always use the -tts suffixed model name for speech generation tasks.

Flash vs Pro: Which Model Should You Choose?

| Comparison | Flash TTS | Pro TTS | |------------|-----------|---------| | Latency | Low (real-time friendly) | Higher | | Voice Quality | High | Maximum | | Expressiveness | Rich | Very Rich | | Best For | Chatbots, live responses | Podcasts, premium content | | Cost | Lower | Higher |

Bottom line: Use Flash for prototyping and real-time use cases; use Pro for final, high-quality deliverables.

FAQ

Q1: Does Gemini TTS support languages other than English?

A: Yes, Gemini TTS supports multiple languages including Japanese, Spanish, French, German, and more. Language quality varies by voice; check the official documentation for language-specific voice recommendations and test a few options to find the best fit for your target language.

Q2: Is there a limit on how much text I can synthesize in one request?

A: Yes. Each TTS API call has an input token limit (typically a few thousand tokens depending on the model). For longer narration, split your text into logical sections and make multiple API calls, then concatenate the resulting audio files using a library like pydub.

Q3: What audio format does the API return?

A: Gemini TTS returns raw PCM audio data — 24kHz sample rate, 16-bit, mono channel. You can wrap it in a WAV container using Python's built-in wave module, or convert it to MP3 or other formats using pydub or ffmpeg.

Conclusion and Next Steps

Gemini 2.5 TTS API delivers a level of expressiveness, pacing control, and dialogue fidelity that far exceeds traditional text-to-speech solutions. The March 2026 enhancements — precision pacing and seamless dialogue — make it a compelling choice for developers building voice-first applications.

To continue exploring the Gemini API, check out these related articles:

  • Gemini Live API Complete Guide: Building Real-Time Voice and Video Conversations
  • Gemini File API Complete Guide: Uploading and Using Files
  • Gemini API JSON Mode and Structured Output Guide

en.title: Gemini CLI Plan Mode Guide: Enhanced Sandboxing for Safe AI Agent Development (March 2026)
en.author: Gemini Lab
en.description: A complete guide to Gemini CLI's new Plan Mode and LXC/gVisor sandbox features. Learn how to build safe, predictable AI agent workflows with structured execution planning and kernel-level isolation.
en.content:
Introduction: Why Gemini CLI Just Got a Lot Safer

In March 2026, Gemini CLI shipped two major features that fundamentally change how developers run AI agents: Plan Mode (now enabled by default) and LXC / gVisor sandbox support. Together, these give you the visibility and isolation you need to confidently delegate complex tasks to an AI — even in sensitive codebases.

This guide walks through how each feature works, how to configure them, and how to integrate them into real development workflows with practical code examples.

What You'll Learn

  • How Plan Mode works and how to customize it
  • Why chat compression no longer breaks your in-progress plans
  • The difference between LXC and gVisor sandboxes — and when to use each
  • Security best practices for production-grade AI agent workflows

Who This Is For

  • Developers already familiar with Gemini CLI basics
  • Engineers who want to run AI agents in CI/CD pipelines safely
  • Anyone building autonomous coding tools with Gemini

What Is Plan Mode?

Plan Mode is a structured workflow feature that makes Gemini CLI present a step-by-step execution plan before taking any action. The developer reviews and approves the plan, then Gemini proceeds.

As of the March 2026 update, Plan Mode is enabled by default — no configuration required.

Why This Matters

Without Plan Mode, AI agents operate reactively: they think and act simultaneously, which can lead to hard-to-predict behavior. Plan Mode solves this by creating a deliberate pause between intent and execution.

User: "Add TypeScript type annotations to all files in src/"

[Gemini CLI – Plan Mode]
📋 Proposed execution plan:
  1. Recursively scan src/ for .ts and .tsx files
  2. Parse AST to identify missing type annotations
  3. Create backups in .backup/ before modifying files
  4. Add type annotations (no `any` types)
  5. Display diff and ask for confirmation
  6. Apply changes after approval

Approve this plan? [y/N]

This transparency gives developers full control over what the AI does — no surprises, no accidental deletions.

Setting Up Plan Mode

Installation and Version Check

Make sure you're on Gemini CLI v0.34.0 or later for full Plan Mode and sandbox support.

Configuring Plan Mode

You can customize Plan Mode behavior in ~/.gemini/config.yaml:

Plan Preservation Through Chat Compression

Long sessions trigger context compression to save tokens. In older versions, this would reset any approved plan mid-task. This is now fixed. Approved plans persist through compression.

Understanding Sandbox Options

The sandbox isolates code and shell commands that Gemini CLI executes, preventing unintended access to your host system. The March 2026 update added two new options: LXC (experimental) and native gVisor (runsc).

Sandbox Comparison

| Feature | none | LXC | gVisor | |---------|------|-----|--------| | Filesystem isolation | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | | Network isolation | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | | Kernel protection | ❌ | Partial | ✅ | | Startup time | Instant | Medium | Fast | | Overhead | None | Low | Very low | | Best for | Dev/Testing | CI/CD | Production / High security |

LXC Sandbox Configuration

LXC provides OS-level virtualization with minimal overhead.

gVisor Sandbox Configuration

gVisor is a user-space kernel developed by Google that intercepts container syscalls, providing strong isolation without full virtualization overhead.

Practical Workflow: Plan Mode + Sandbox

Here's a real-world example: migrating an Express.js API server to Fastify, safely.

Running the Migration

What you'll see in the terminal:

📋 Gemini CLI – Execution Plan
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Task: Express → Fastify migration

Steps:
  [1] Parse package.json to identify Express dependencies
  [2] Scan all JS files in src/
  [3] Identify Express routing patterns (estimated: 12 locations)
  [4] Convert to Fastify plugin architecture
  [5] Update test files in __tests__/
  [6] Run npm test to verify

Files affected (estimated):
  📝 src/server.js
  📝 src/routes/user.js
  📝 src/routes/product.js
  📝 __tests__/server.test.js

Sandbox: gVisor (kernel-protected)
Estimated time: 2–3 minutes

Approve this plan? [y/N] y

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
✅ Step 1/6: package.json analyzed
✅ Step 2/6: 15 files scanned
🔄 Step 3/6: Detecting Express patterns...

Automating with Python

For CI/CD pipelines, you can drive Plan Mode programmatically:

Common Errors and Fixes

Error 1: gVisor not found

Error: gVisor sandbox requested but 'runsc' not found in PATH

Fix:

Error 2: Plan resets after chat compression

This was a bug in v0.33 and earlier. Update to the latest version:

Error 3: LXC container fails to start

Error: LXC container failed to start (permission denied)

Fix:

Security Best Practices

1. Least-Privilege Networking

Only allow the specific hosts your task needs:

2. CI/CD Integration

FAQ

Q1. Can I disable Plan Mode?

Yes. Though we recommend keeping it on for production workflows:

Q2. Does gVisor work on macOS?

Not natively — gVisor is Linux-only as of March 2026. On macOS, use LXC sandbox or run via Docker Desktop with gVisor support. Check the Gemini CLI docs for the latest platform support.

Q3. Can I auto-approve in CI/CD?

Yes, use --auto-approve. For more granular control, set auto_approve_low_risk: true in your config — this automatically approves file reads and comment additions while still prompting for file deletions or external API calls.

Q4. Can sandboxes access GPUs?

gVisor has experimental CUDA passthrough support, but it requires manual configuration. LXC supports GPU passthrough natively and is the better choice for AI inference tasks running locally.

Q5. Which model does Plan Mode use?

Plan Mode works with both Gemini 3.1 Pro and Gemini 3.1 Flash-Lite. Gemini 3.1 Pro generates more detailed multi-step plans and handles complex migrations better. For simple, high-volume tasks, Flash-Lite offers better cost efficiency without sacrificing plan quality.

Conclusion and Next Steps

Plan Mode and the new sandboxing options represent a meaningful step forward in making AI-assisted development both safe and predictable.

  • Plan Mode makes agent intent visible before execution — no more surprises
  • Plan preservation ensures long tasks survive context compression intact
  • gVisor delivers kernel-level isolation with minimal overhead, ideal for security-sensitive environments
  • LXC is the right choice for CI/CD pipelines and GPU-intensive workloads

Ready to go deeper? Check out these related guides:

  • Getting Started with Gemini CLI
  • Running Gemini Agents in Production
  • Gemini API Function Calling Deep Dive

en.title: Gemini Code Assist Complete Guide: Agent Mode, Finish Changes & Outlines (2026)
en.author: Gemini Lab
en.description: Master Gemini Code Assist's latest features: Agent Mode for autonomous multi-step tasks, Finish Changes for intent-based completion, and Outlines for instant code comprehension — 2026 edition.
en.content:
What Is Gemini Code Assist? Your AI Pair Programmer in 2026

Gemini Code Assist is Google's AI-powered coding assistant, deeply integrated into VS Code, IntelliJ IDEA, and other major IDEs. It started as a smart autocomplete tool, but in 2026 it has evolved into something far more powerful: a full-fledged AI pair programmer capable of autonomously executing complex, multi-file tasks.

Three features define this new era of Gemini Code Assist:

  • Agent Mode — Autonomously executes multi-step coding tasks across your entire project
  • Finish Changes — Reads your in-progress edits and completes them without requiring a prompt
  • Outlines — Displays high-level code summaries directly in your editor

In this guide, we'll walk through installation, configuration, and practical use cases for each of these features so you can start shipping code faster today.

Installation and Setup

VS Code Installation

Install Gemini Code Assist directly from the VS Code Marketplace:

  1. Open the Extensions panel (Ctrl+Shift+X / Cmd+Shift+X)
  2. Search for "Gemini Code Assist" and click Install
  3. Sign in with your Google account when prompted

You can also install via the command line:

IntelliJ IDEA Installation

For IntelliJ-based IDEs:

  1. Go to File > Settings > Plugins (macOS: IntelliJ IDEA > Preferences > Plugins)
  2. Switch to the Marketplace tab and search for "Gemini Code Assist"
  3. Click Install and restart the IDE
  4. Sign in with your Google account from the Gemini icon in the status bar

Choosing Your Edition

Gemini Code Assist comes in three editions:

| Edition | Best For | Key Features | |---------|----------|-------------| | Free | Individual developers | Code completion, chat, basic Agent Mode | | Standard | Teams & businesses | Enterprise security, larger context | | Enterprise | Large organizations | Private code customization, Cloud integration |

Agent Mode: Autonomous Multi-Step Tasks

What Agent Mode Can Do

Agent Mode transforms Gemini Code Assist from a reactive assistant into a proactive collaborator. Instead of answering one question at a time, Agent Mode can:

  • Edit multiple files in a single session
  • Create new files and update imports automatically
  • Run shell commands (with your approval)
  • Integrate with ecosystem tools via MCP
  • Maintain full project context throughout the session

Activating Agent Mode

In VS Code:

# Open the chat panel
Ctrl+Shift+P → "Gemini: Open Chat"

# Toggle Agent Mode in the chat input bar
# Or use the /agent command directly
/agent Add JWT authentication to this Express app with protected routes

A Real-World Example: Adding Authentication

Here's what Agent Mode generates when asked to add JWT authentication to an Express project:

Before making any changes, Agent Mode presents a plan for your review. You can approve, modify, or reject each proposed change — giving you full control over what the AI does.

Auto Approve Mode

For trusted workflows, enable Auto Approve to let the agent act without confirmation:

Best practice: Reserve Auto Approve for well-tested, low-risk workflows. Always review the git diff after an agent session and run your test suite before committing.

Finish Changes: Code Completion Without Prompts

How Finish Changes Works

Finish Changes is powered by Gemini 3.0 and takes a fundamentally different approach to code completion. Rather than predicting the next token, it observes your editing patterns, understands your intent from partial code and comments, and completes the entire intended change.

No chat, no prompts — just keep coding and let Gemini catch up.

Pattern Replication

Demonstrate a repetitive change once, and Finish Changes applies the same pattern everywhere:

Inline TODO Instructions

Write instructions as comments and let Finish Changes implement them:

This is particularly useful for handling code review feedback — paste a reviewer's comment as a TODO, and let Finish Changes implement the fix.

Outlines: Understand Code at a Glance

What Outlines Does

The Outlines feature generates concise English summaries of your code and displays them directly in the editor, interleaved with the source. Unlike explanations in a side panel, these summaries appear exactly where they're relevant — next to the code they describe.

This dramatically reduces ramp-up time when working in unfamiliar codebases.

Outlines in Practice

Here's how Outlines annotates a complex React component:

Context Drawer: Precision Context Management

The Context Drawer gives you fine-grained control over what Gemini can see during a conversation. Rather than feeding the AI your entire monorepo, you can:

# Open Context Drawer
Click the "Context" icon in the chat panel header

# Typical workflows:
# - Add a specific config file for infrastructure questions
# - Remove the /legacy folder to avoid outdated patterns
# - Temporarily include a reference implementation from another package

Precise context control leads to more accurate, relevant responses — especially in large, multi-package projects.

Custom Slash Commands

Create reusable commands for your team's recurring workflows:

After saving this file, /test, /review, and /doc become available directly in the chat panel.

MCP Integration for Extended Capabilities

Gemini Code Assist supports the Model Context Protocol (MCP), allowing it to connect to external tools and data sources:

With MCP configured, Gemini can pull GitHub Issues into context, query your database schema, and generate code that accurately reflects your actual data structures. See the Gemini MCP Integration Guide for a complete walkthrough.

Frequently Asked Questions

Q1. How does Gemini Code Assist compare to Gemini CLI?

Gemini Code Assist lives inside your IDE and is optimized for interactive, GUI-based development workflows — think chat, inline suggestions, and visual diffs. Gemini CLI is a terminal-first tool built for scripting, CI/CD pipelines, and shell-based automation. Both tools run Gemini 2.5 under the hood in 2026. Most developers end up using both: Code Assist during active development sessions, and CLI for automating repetitive tasks.

Q2. Is it safe to use Agent Mode on a production codebase?

Agent Mode is designed with safety in mind — by default, it always shows you a plan before making changes. That said, best practices apply: work on a feature branch, review the git diff after every agent session, and run your full test suite before merging. For critical systems, avoid Auto Approve mode and treat agent-generated code the same way you'd treat code from a junior developer: review it carefully before it ships.

Q3. What's the difference between Finish Changes and standard autocomplete?

Standard autocomplete predicts the next few tokens based on the cursor position. Finish Changes understands your intent across the entire file — it can propagate a refactoring change to every relevant location, implement a TODO comment you wrote in plain English, or complete a partially written function based on its name, parameters, and surrounding context. It's less like tab-complete and more like having someone read over your shoulder and finishing your thoughts.

Q4. Can Gemini Code Assist be customized for my company's coding standards?

Yes, with the Enterprise edition. You can connect your private code repositories so that Gemini learns your team's patterns, naming conventions, and architectural preferences. For all editions, custom slash commands (.gemini/commands.json) let you encode your team's standard workflows into reusable prompts that anyone on the team can invoke.

Wrapping Up: Gemini Code Assist in 2026

Gemini Code Assist has matured from a smart autocomplete tool into a comprehensive AI development platform. Agent Mode handles the tedious parts of software development so you can focus on architecture and problem-solving. Finish Changes keeps you in flow by completing your thoughts without interrupting you for a prompt. And Outlines makes diving into unfamiliar code feel less daunting.

The common thread across all of these features is that you stay in control. AI proposes, you decide. That balance is what makes Gemini Code Assist a practical tool for professional development — not just an impressive demo.

If you're ready to go deeper with the Gemini ecosystem, check out the Gemini API Quickstart Guide to start building your own Gemini-powered applications.

en.title: Lyria RealTime API: Build Infinite Music Streams with Gemini AI
en.author: Gemini Lab
en.description: Learn how to generate real-time, continuous music streams using Google DeepMind's Lyria RealTime API. Includes WebSocket setup, BPM control, and Python code examples.
en.content:
What Is Lyria RealTime?

Lyria RealTime is Google DeepMind's real-time music generation model, available within the Gemini API ecosystem. Unlike conventional AI music tools that generate a fixed audio clip, Lyria RealTime produces a continuous, theoretically infinite music stream via WebSocket — and it lets you adjust the style, tempo, and density of that stream on the fly.

As of March 2026, the model is offered as a free experimental API, making it an attractive choice for developers building games, interactive installations, live performance tools, or any application that needs dynamic, AI-generated background music.

In this tutorial, you'll learn everything you need to go from zero to a working real-time music generation system in Python.

Key Capabilities

What makes Lyria RealTime stand out from other AI music generators:

  • Continuous streaming: Up to 10 minutes of uninterrupted music per session
  • Real-time parameter control: Adjust BPM, density, and style while music plays; changes take effect within 2 seconds
  • High-fidelity audio: 48 kHz stereo PCM stream
  • SynthID watermarking: All generated audio is embedded with an imperceptible digital watermark for provenance tracking
  • Free experimental access: No billing required while in experimental status

Prerequisites

What You'll Need

  • Python 3.9 or higher
  • A Google AI Studio API key (free at aistudio.google.com)
  • google-genai SDK and optional audio playback library

Installation

Configure Your API Key

Model Reference

| Model ID | Description | |----------|-------------| | models/lyria-realtime-exp | Experimental real-time music generation model |

WebSocket Endpoint:

wss://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/ws/google.ai.generativelanguage.v1alpha.GenerativeService.BidiGenerateMusic

The Python SDK handles the WebSocket lifecycle for you — no need to manage the low-level connection manually.

Step 1: Generate Music and Save to a WAV File

Let's start with the simplest use case: generate 30 seconds of music from a text prompt and save it as a WAV file.

Step 2: Real-Time Parameter Control

The real power of Lyria RealTime is the ability to change music parameters during an active session. The example below walks through three distinct musical phases within a single session.

Configuration Reference

LiveMusicGenerationConfig Parameters

| Parameter | Type | Range | Default | Description | |-----------|------|-------|---------|-------------| | bpm | int | 60–200 | — | Beats per minute (tempo) | | density | float | 0.0–1.0 | — | Note density; 0.0 = sparse, 1.0 = complex | | guidance | float | 0.0–6.0 | 4.0 | How closely to follow the prompt |

Blending Multiple Prompts

You can blend two musical styles with weighted prompts:

When to Call reset_context()

A context reset is required when you change:

  • BPM — resets the rhythmic pattern foundation
  • Musical scale or key — resets tonal context

Prompt changes and density adjustments do not require a reset.

Common Errors and Fixes

Error: WebSocket Connection Refused

ConnectionRefusedError: WebSocket connection to generativelanguage.googleapis.com failed

Fix: Verify your API key is valid and that you're passing http_options={"api_version": "v1alpha"} to the client.

Error: Session Timeout After 10 Minutes

Lyria RealTime sessions are capped at 10 minutes. For longer-running applications, implement a reconnection loop:

Error: Parameters Not Taking Effect

Always call play() after set_music_generation_config() to apply the new settings.

Real-World Use Case: Adaptive Game Soundtrack

Here's a practical example showing how Lyria RealTime can drive a game's adaptive soundtrack:

FAQ

Q1. Can I use Lyria RealTime for commercial projects?

As an experimental API, usage is governed by Google's API terms of service. All generated audio carries an embedded SynthID watermark. Review the latest terms at ai.google.dev before incorporating it into commercial products.

Q2. What's the difference between Lyria 3 and Lyria RealTime?

Lyria 3 is a consumer-facing model available in the Gemini app that produces polished 30-second tracks (with vocals, MP3/MP4 output). Lyria RealTime is a developer API that generates a continuous PCM audio stream over WebSocket, optimized for interactive and dynamic use cases. A public Lyria 3 API is not yet available as of March 2026.

Q3. Should I write prompts in English?

Yes — English prompts consistently produce better results. Instead of vague single words, describe the genre, instrumentation, mood, and rhythm in detail. For example: "smooth lo-fi hip-hop with slow piano chords, vinyl crackle, and mellow bass" will outperform just "lo-fi music".

Q4. Can I use Lyria RealTime in a mobile app?

Direct WebSocket connections from mobile clients are possible in theory, but a server-side architecture is recommended: run Lyria RealTime on a backend, then stream the generated audio to your mobile client. This also simplifies API key management.

Q5. Does Lyria RealTime support vocals?

Not currently. Lyria RealTime is designed for instrumental music generation. For vocal tracks, use Lyria 3 through the Gemini app.

Conclusion

Lyria RealTime opens up a creative space that few AI music tools have explored: real-time, controllable music streaming that can adapt to context and user input as it plays. Whether you're building a game, an interactive art installation, or a DJ tool, the API provides the primitives you need.

Key takeaways from this tutorial:

  • Connect using the models/lyria-realtime-exp model via the Python SDK
  • Control BPM (60–200), density (0.0–1.0), and guidance (0.0–6.0) in real time
  • Call reset_context() after BPM or scale changes
  • Sessions are limited to 10 minutes — implement reconnection for longer use
  • Blend multiple prompt styles with weighted prompts for nuanced music

To go deeper into Gemini's audio capabilities, check out the Gemini Audio Understanding Guide, the Gemini Live API Guide, and the Gemini TTS API Guide for text-to-speech integration.

en.title: Using Cloud Storage with the Gemini API: Bucket Integration and 100MB File Input Guide
en.author: Gemini Lab
en.description: A complete guide to the Gemini API's new Cloud Storage integration. Learn how to use GCS buckets and presigned URLs for up to 100MB file input with Python and JavaScript code examples.
en.content:
Introduction: Gemini API's Enhanced Cloud Storage Integration

In March 2026, Google significantly upgraded the Gemini API's file input capabilities. In addition to the existing File API (limited to 20MB), you can now specify Google Cloud Storage (GCS) buckets and presigned URLs directly as data sources. The file size limit has also been raised to 100MB.

This update opens the door to new use cases, including:

  • Analyzing large video files stored in GCS with Gemini
  • Using presigned URLs from S3-compatible or Azure Blob Storage
  • Building enterprise-scale pipelines with Vertex AI integration

This guide walks you through setting up Cloud Storage integration and provides practical code examples.

Prerequisites and Setup

What You Need

  • Python 3.9+ or Node.js 18+
  • A Google Cloud project (for GCS usage)
  • Gemini API key (get one at Google AI Studio)
  • google-genai package (v1.x or later)

Configure Your API Key

How Cloud Storage Integration Works

Comparing the Old File API vs. New Cloud Storage Integration

| Feature | Legacy File API | New Cloud Storage Integration | |---------|----------------|-------------------------------| | File size limit | 20MB | 100MB | | Storage | Google servers (temporary) | GCS buckets / any presigned URL | | URL support | None | GCS URIs & signed HTTPS URLs | | Persistence | Auto-deleted after 48 hours | Managed by your bucket | | Cost | API transfer cost | GCS storage + transfer cost |

Supported Input Formats

The new feature supports the following file reference formats:

  1. GCS URI (e.g., gs://my-bucket/my-video.mp4)
  2. Public URL (any directly accessible HTTPS file)
  3. Presigned URL (a time-limited signed URL for private access)

Implementation: Python with Cloud Storage

Method 1: Directly Specify a GCS URI

The simplest approach — reference a GCS file directly in your request.

Method 2: Use a Presigned URL

Access files from AWS S3, Azure Blob Storage, or any other cloud storage service.

Method 3: Processing Large Files Up to 100MB

Take advantage of the new 100MB limit for large video or document analysis.

Implementation: JavaScript with Cloud Storage

Use GCS files with Gemini from a Node.js environment.

GCS Bucket Permissions Setup

For Gemini API to access a GCS bucket, you need to configure the appropriate IAM permissions.

Setting Up a Service Account

Configure Authentication

Using Vertex AI? When accessing Gemini through Vertex AI, GCS authentication is handled automatically via Workload Identity. See the Vertex AI Quickstart Guide for details.

Common Errors and Solutions

Error 1: Permission Denied

PermissionDenied: 403 GET https://storage.googleapis.com/.../file.pdf:
ACCESS_DENIED

Fix: Ensure the service account used by Gemini API has the storage.objectViewer role on the bucket. Check the IAM policy in the Google Cloud Console.

Error 2: File Size Exceeded

InvalidArgument: 400 File size exceeds the limit of 100MB

Fix: The file exceeds the 100MB limit. For videos, reduce resolution or split into segments. For PDFs, extract only the relevant pages.

Error 3: Unsupported MIME Type

InvalidArgument: Unsupported MIME type

Fix: Make sure the mime_type parameter is correct. Common MIME types:

| File Type | MIME Type | |-----------|-----------| | PDF | application/pdf | | MP4 Video | video/mp4 | | JPEG Image | image/jpeg | | PNG Image | image/png | | MP3 Audio | audio/mp3 | | WAV Audio | audio/wav | | Plain Text | text/plain |

Error 4: Presigned URL Expired

InvalidArgument: URL has expired

Fix: Check the expiration time of your presigned URL. Account for Gemini's processing time (typically 1–3 minutes) and set an expiration of at least 30 minutes.

Advanced: Enterprise-Scale Pipeline

A scalable document processing pipeline combining GCS and Gemini via Cloud Functions.

This pattern is useful for automating tasks like:

  • Contract and invoice data extraction
  • Automatic product image tagging for e-commerce
  • Meeting recording transcription and summarization
  • Security camera footage anomaly detection

For production deployment on Vertex AI, see the Vertex AI Gemini Production Guide.

File API vs. Cloud Storage: When to Use Each

| Use Case | Recommended Approach | |----------|---------------------| | Temporary files under 20MB | File API | | Large files from 20MB to 100MB | Cloud Storage integration (new) | | Systems integrated with GCP | Direct GCS URI | | Files on AWS S3 or Azure Blob | Presigned URL | | Files to reuse across requests | GCS + URI reference | | Prototyping and testing | File API |

FAQ

Q1. Do I need Vertex AI to use Cloud Storage integration?

A. No. GCS URIs work with both the Gemini Developer API (API key) and Vertex AI. However, Vertex AI is recommended for enterprise use cases as it integrates seamlessly with Google Cloud's IAM and Workload Identity.

Q2. What are the security implications of using presigned URLs?

A. The Gemini API server fetches the file from the presigned URL. Keep expiration times short (30 minutes to 1 hour is recommended) and revoke URLs after use. Always store your API key in environment variables or Secret Manager — never hardcode it.

Q3. How do I handle files larger than 100MB?

A. Files exceeding 100MB cannot be processed directly. For videos, use FFmpeg to split or reduce resolution. For PDFs, extract only the relevant pages. For very large datasets, consider extracting keyframes from videos for multimodal processing, or use Vertex AI's Long Context capabilities.

Q4. Can Gemini access private GCS buckets?

A. Yes. Grant the storage.objectViewer role to the service account used by the Gemini API. When using Vertex AI, Workload Identity provides a more secure, keyless authentication approach.

Q5. Which file formats are supported?

A. Supported formats include PDFs, images (JPEG, PNG, WebP, HEIC), videos (MP4, MOV, AVI, etc.), audio (MP3, WAV, FLAC, etc.), and text files (TXT, HTML, Markdown, CSV, etc.). Note that video and audio processing requires a multimodal-capable model such as Gemini 3 Pro or Gemini 3 Flash.

Conclusion

The Gemini API's Cloud Storage integration is a significant upgrade that streamlines enterprise AI workflows. Here's a summary of the key takeaways:

  • File size limit raised to 100MB (up from 20MB)
  • GCS URIs can be used directly — no need to re-upload files
  • Presigned URLs enable cross-cloud integration with AWS S3 and Azure Blob
  • Cloud Functions integration enables scalable event-driven pipelines

Start by grabbing an API key from Google AI Studio and passing a GCS file directly to Gemini. It's a powerful capability that integrates naturally with your existing cloud infrastructure.

For advanced multimodal processing, check out Building a Multimodal RAG System with Gemini. For File API basics, the Gemini File API Complete Guide is a great starting point.

en.title: Building Enterprise-Grade Gemini AI Platforms — From Multimodal Integration to Production Operations
en.author: Gemini Lab
en.description: Complete guide to building enterprise-scale AI platforms with Gemini API. Covers multimodal input processing, intelligent caching, error handling, scaling strategies, security, and production monitoring with code examples.
en.content:
Building Enterprise-Grade Gemini AI Platforms

Introduction

When organizations deploy Gemini API at scale, simple API calls are insufficient. This article provides a complete roadmap for designing and building enterprise-grade AI platforms.

We cover multimodal input processing (text, images, video), intelligent caching strategies, robust error handling, horizontal scaling, security measures, monitoring/logging, and cost optimization with detailed implementation examples.

Architecture Overview

Core Components of an Enterprise Platform

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│             Frontend (Web / Mobile / API)              │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│   Auth Layer (OAuth 2.0, API Key Management)          │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│   API Gateway (Rate Limiting, Request Validation)      │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│   Middleware (Logging, Telemetry, Caching)             │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│   Gemini Integration Layer (Model Selection, Routing)  │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│   Data Processing (Multimodal, Cleanup, Enrichment)    │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│   Distributed Cache (Redis / Memcached)                │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│   Database Layer (Metadata, History, Audit Logs)       │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│   Monitoring & Analytics (Prometheus, Datadog)         │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

This architecture achieves high availability, scalability, and security simultaneously.

Part 1: Building the Multimodal Processing Engine

Image & Video Preprocessing and Optimization

Part 2: Intelligent Caching Strategy

Hierarchical Cache Architecture

Part 3: Production Error Handling & Retry Logic

Part 4: Scaling Strategies

Batch Processing with Async Workers

Part 5: Security & Audit

API Key Management and Request Validation

Part 6: Monitoring & Observability

Production-Grade Logging

Part 7: Integrated Implementation Example

Complete Enterprise Workflow

FAQ

Q1: How is the cache key determined?

A: We generate SHA-256 hash from model, input content, and key config parameters. Same input always produces same key.

Q2: How many retries are performed?

A: Default is 3 with exponential backoff: 1s, 2s, 4s waits.

Q3: What is the circuit breaker's role?

A: It prevents excessive load during failures by temporarily blocking API calls after consecutive errors.

Q4: Which errors trigger retries?

A: HTTP 429 (rate limit), 502/503/504 (server errors). Auth errors do not retry.

Q5: Cost optimization tips?

A: Maximize cache hit rate, use Gemini 2.5 Flash, compress inputs, batch processing, monitor token usage.

Conclusion

Running Gemini API at enterprise scale requires much more than simple API calls. Combining the components covered in this article—multimodal processing, intelligent caching, robust error handling, scaling strategies, security, and comprehensive monitoring—you can build a production-ready AI platform.

Implement these elements progressively and customize for your organization's needs.

en.title: Gemini 2.5 Pro Extended Thinking Mode — Deeper Reasoning for High-Precision Answers
en.author: Gemini Lab
en.description: Learn how Gemini 2.5 Pro's Extended Thinking mode enhances reasoning capabilities. Explore the mechanism behind deeper inference, ideal use cases, and implementation methods with code examples for complex problem-solving.
en.content:
Gemini 2.5 Pro Extended Thinking Mode

Introduction

Gemini 2.5 Pro's Extended Thinking is a feature that deepens the AI's reasoning process, generating more accurate and well-founded answers to complex problems. Rather than providing instant responses, it takes time to analyze problems step-by-step, considers multiple approaches, and derives the final answer through comprehensive evaluation.

This article explains the mechanism of Extended Thinking, applicable use cases, implementation methods, and API usage with code examples.

What is Extended Thinking?

Extended Thinking mode allows Gemini 2.5 Pro to perform an "invisible reasoning process" for a given problem. This process follows these steps:

  1. Problem Analysis: Break down the given problem into components
  2. Hypothesis Generation: Consider multiple approaches
  3. Reasoning: Verify the pros and cons of each approach
  4. Conclusion: Generate the optimal answer

This entire process is exposed to API users as "thinking" and can be referenced alongside the final answer.

Differences from Standard Mode

| Aspect | Standard Mode | Extended Thinking | |--------|--------------|-------------------| | Reasoning Time | Short (few seconds) | Long (10-60 seconds) | | Accuracy | High (usually sufficient) | Higher (for complex problems) | | Cost | Lower | Slightly higher | | Use Cases | Text generation, Q&A | Math, code, logic problems |

Applicable Use Cases

Extended Thinking is particularly effective for complex problem-solving scenarios:

✓ Ideal Use Cases

  • Mathematical & Physics Calculations: Multi-step computations and proofs
  • Code Generation & Debugging: Complex algorithm implementation
  • Logic Problems: Puzzles and constraint satisfaction problems
  • Technical Design: Architecture decisions and best practice selection
  • Data Analysis: Statistical analysis and trend prediction

✗ Not Suitable For

  • Template or boilerplate generation
  • Simple information retrieval
  • Real-time chat responses

Basic API Usage

Step 1: Environment Setup

Step 2: Basic Extended Thinking Request

Step 3: Solving More Complex Problems

Practical Example: Complex Algorithm Problem

Solve the classic "3Sum" problem using Extended Thinking:

Parameter Details

thinking Object

| Parameter | Type | Description | |-----------|------|-------------| | type | string | Set to "enabled" to activate Extended Thinking. Default is "disabled" | | budget_tokens | int | Maximum tokens allocated for reasoning (1,000–100,000) |

Larger budget_tokens values enable deeper thinking but increase response time and cost. Recommended range: 5,000–15,000.

FAQ

Q1: Which models support Extended Thinking?

A: Available on the latest Gemini 2.5 Pro version. Gemini 2.5 Flash does not currently support this feature.

Q2: Is there a limit to thinking process length?

A: Controlled by the budget_tokens parameter. Default is 5,000 tokens, maximum 100,000.

Q3: Does Extended Thinking increase costs?

A: Yes. Tokens used in the thinking process are billable. Use only when complex problem-solving is necessary.

Q4: Can thinking be hidden?

A: The API always returns thinking. UI implementations may offer hide options.

Q5: Does Extended Thinking work with non-English languages?

A: Yes. Japanese and other languages are fully supported for both reasoning and responses.

Best Practices

  1. Enable only for complex problems: Use standard mode for simple questions
  2. Set budget_tokens appropriately: Adjust within 5,000–15,000 based on problem complexity
  3. Leverage thinking output: Review the reasoning to understand answer justification
  4. Implement error handling: Account for potential timeouts with retry logic

Conclusion

Gemini 2.5 Pro's Extended Thinking mode significantly improves AI accuracy and reliability for complex problem-solving. Whether you're tackling mathematical calculations, code generation, or logic problems, Extended Thinking delivers well-founded, high-precision answers. The API is straightforward—just configure the thinking parameter.

Try Extended Thinking in your projects today!

en.title: Gemini API Multi-Turn Chat Guide — Conversation History, Context Management & Chatbot Building
en.author: Gemini Lab
en.description: Learn how to build conversational AI with Gemini API's multi-turn chat feature. Covers conversation history management, context persistence, System Instructions integration, and practical chatbot examples.
en.content:
What Is Multi-Turn Chat?

A standard generateContent() call is a one-shot interaction — each request is independent. But chat applications need the AI to maintain context across multiple exchanges. Gemini API's startChat() method handles this automatically, preserving conversation history so each response is informed by everything said before.

This article walks through multi-turn chat basics, history management, System Instructions integration, and building a practical chatbot step by step.

Basic Multi-Turn Chat

Simple Conversation Implementation

The chat session created by start_chat() automatically accumulates all sent and received messages in the history property. The second question about "each approach" is correctly interpreted because the first Q&A is preserved as context.

Starting a Chat with Existing History

To resume a previous conversation, pass a history parameter to start_chat().

This feature lets you load conversation history from a database and seamlessly resume sessions.

Combining with System Instructions

Set up the AI's personality and response rules with System Instructions, then combine with multi-turn chat to create a consistently behaved chatbot.

System Instructions apply to the entire conversation, so the "TechShop support agent" behavior persists regardless of how many turns have passed. For more on this, see the Prompt Engineering Guide.

Streaming for Real-Time Responses

Chat UIs typically display text as it's generated rather than waiting for the full response. This is called streaming.

Passing stream=True to send_message() returns the response in chunks. Printing each chunk immediately gives users a natural experience of the AI "thinking as it speaks."

Saving and Loading Conversations

Production chat apps need the ability to save conversations and resume them later.

Managing Token Usage

Token consumption grows as conversations get longer in multi-turn chat. Use count_tokens() to monitor usage proactively.

FAQ

Q1. What's the difference between multi-turn chat and single generateContent() calls?

A1. generateContent() treats each call independently with no memory of previous interactions. Multi-turn chat via startChat() automatically includes the full conversation history in every request, enabling context-aware responses that reference earlier messages.

Q2. How should I handle very long conversation histories?

A2. Three approaches work well: (1) Keep only the most recent N messages. (2) Use Gemini to summarize older messages and compress token usage. (3) Monitor token count with count_tokens() and start a new session when a threshold is reached. Combine these strategies based on your use case.

Q3. Can I use streaming with multi-turn chat?

A3. Yes, pass stream=True to chat.send_message() to enable streaming. It works the same way as streaming with regular generateContent, returning chunks that you can display incrementally. See Streaming & Chat for more details.

Q4. Can I send images or videos within a chat session?

A4. Yes, Gemini supports multimodal input in chat sessions. Include image data in the parts array of your message to ask questions about images while maintaining the full conversation context. See the Multimodal API Guide for details.

Summary

Gemini API's multi-turn chat uses a simple pattern: create a session with startChat(), then exchange messages with send_message(). With automatic history management, System Instructions integration, and streaming support, you can build practical chatbots with minimal code.

Start with a basic chat implementation, then layer in conversation persistence and token management as your application requires. For API fundamentals, see the Gemini API Quickstart. For prompt techniques, check out the Prompt Engineering Guide.

en.title: Gemini × Firebase Integration Guide — Build AI Apps with Firestore & Cloud Functions
en.author: Gemini Lab
en.description: Learn how to build AI-powered apps by combining Gemini API with Firebase. Covers Firestore data persistence, Cloud Functions API calls, and security configuration with practical examples.
en.content:
Why Combine Gemini with Firebase?

While the Gemini API alone can generate text and analyze images, real-world applications need user management, data persistence, and server-side processing. Firebase provides all of these as fully managed services, and pairing it with Gemini lets you build production-grade AI apps quickly.

This article walks through building a backend that safely calls the Gemini API using Firebase's Firestore (database) and Cloud Functions (serverless functions).

Prerequisites and Setup

What You Need

  • Node.js 18 or later installed
  • A Firebase project created (Firebase Console)
  • A Gemini API key from Google AI Studio (how to get one)
  • Firebase CLI installed

Setting Up Firebase CLI

Calling the Gemini API from Cloud Functions

Secure API Key Management

Never hardcode your Gemini API key in source code. Use Firebase's secrets management to store it securely.

Basic Cloud Function Implementation

Storing Conversation History in Firestore

For AI chat applications, preserving conversation history is essential for maintaining context across interactions. Firestore enables real-time data synchronization out of the box.

Firestore Security Rules

Use Firestore security rules to ensure users can only access their own data.

// firestore.rules
rules_version = '2';
service cloud.firestore {
  match /databases/{database}/documents {

    // User conversation data
    match /users/{userId}/conversations/{convId} {
      // Only the owner can read and write
      allow read, write: if request.auth != null
                         && request.auth.uid == userId;

      // Messages subcollection
      match /messages/{messageId} {
        allow read: if request.auth != null
                    && request.auth.uid == userId;
        // Write access restricted to Cloud Functions only
        allow write: if false;
      }
    }
  }
}

The key design decision here is blocking client-side writes to the messages subcollection and routing all writes through Cloud Functions. This prevents users from injecting arbitrary data directly into their conversation history.

Calling from the Frontend (Web)

Deployment and Testing

The emulator lets you test locally without consuming actual Firebase resources. Note that the Gemini API itself is not emulated, so you still need a real API key during testing.

Production Best Practices

Here are key considerations when running Gemini API calls in Cloud Functions.

Rate limiting: Set instance caps on your Cloud Functions to prevent excessive API calls. Use the maxInstances: 10 option to control concurrency.

Cost management: Gemini API charges accrue with each request. Track per-user daily request counts in Firestore and reject requests once the limit is reached.

Error handling: Implement retry logic for API timeouts and transient errors. Don't forget to set timeoutSeconds: 60 for functions that call external APIs.

Caching: Cache Gemini responses in Firestore for identical prompts to reduce API call volume and latency.

FAQ

Q1. Can I run a Gemini API app on Firebase's free tier?

A1. Firebase's Spark plan (free tier) doesn't allow outbound network calls from Cloud Functions. You need the Blaze plan (pay-as-you-go) to call external APIs like Gemini. However, the Blaze plan still includes free-tier allowances, so small-scale development and testing costs very little.

Q2. Should I use Vertex AI or Google AI Studio API keys?

A2. For prototypes and personal projects, Google AI Studio API keys are the quickest path. For production environments or enterprise use, Vertex AI offers IAM-based access control and region selection. See Vertex AI Quickstart for details.

Q3. Won't conversation history hit the token limit as it grows?

A3. Gemini 2.0 Flash supports a 1 million token context window, but longer histories increase costs. Use limit(50) as shown in the example to cap recent messages, or generate summaries to compress older conversation history.

Q4. How does this differ from the Firebase Extensions "Gemini API" extension?

A4. Firebase Extensions offer a no-code setup, but with limited customization. The Cloud Functions approach in this article gives you full control over prompt engineering, history management, rate limiting, and error handling — making it better suited for production applications.

Summary

Combining Gemini API with Firebase gives you authentication, database storage, and serverless functions — everything needed for a production AI application. The architecture of managing API keys securely in Cloud Functions while persisting conversation history in Firestore forms a solid foundation for chatbots and AI assistants.

Pair this with other Firebase services like Authentication, Hosting, and Cloud Storage to build a complete full-stack AI app entirely on the Firebase platform. Start with the Gemini API Quickstart to learn the API basics, then try the architecture described here.

en.title: Gemini Prompt Engineering Guide — System Instructions, Few-shot & Chain-of-Thought
en.author: Gemini Lab
en.description: Learn how to get high-quality output from Gemini through prompt engineering. Covers System Instructions, Few-shot prompting, and Chain-of-Thought with practical examples.
en.content:
What Is Prompt Engineering?

Prompt engineering is the practice of optimizing how you write instructions for AI models. The same Gemini model can produce dramatically different results depending on how you structure your prompts.

While Gemini's multimodal capabilities (text, images, audio, video) are a major strength, mastering text prompts is the essential foundation. This article covers three core techniques: System Instructions, Few-shot prompting, and Chain-of-Thought reasoning.

Using System Instructions

System Instructions control Gemini's behavior across an entire conversation. You can set them in Google AI Studio or via the API, and they apply consistently to every response.

Setting Up in Google AI Studio

In Google AI Studio, enter your instructions in the "System Instructions" panel on the left. Specify the model's role, output format, and any constraints.

Setting Up via API

Effective System Instructions include three key elements: role definition ("You are a ... expert"), output format specification ("in bullet points", "as JSON"), and constraint setting ("under 300 words", "in English").

Few-shot Prompting

Few-shot prompting provides 2–5 examples of desired output to guide the model's response pattern. It's especially effective for teaching the model new task formats.

The key to effective few-shot prompting is example diversity. Include examples covering all expected output patterns (positive, negative, neutral) to prevent biased responses.

Chain-of-Thought (CoT) Reasoning
