en.title: Complete Guide to Gemini API System Instructions — Control AI Behavior Like a Pro
en.author: Gemini Lab
en.description: Learn how to use Gemini API System Instructions to control your AI's persona, tone, and output format. Includes practical Python and JavaScript examples with real-world use cases.
en.content:
Complete Guide to Gemini API System Instructions — Control AI Behavior Like a Pro

Introduction

If you've been building applications with the Gemini API, you've likely noticed that raw prompts alone often aren't enough to make your AI behave consistently. That's where System Instructions come in.

System Instructions let you define the model's persona, communication style, output format, and behavioral constraints before any user interaction takes place. Once set, these instructions persist across the entire conversation — even if the user tries to steer the model elsewhere.

In this guide, you'll learn:

  • What System Instructions are and how they differ from regular prompts
  • How to implement them in Python and JavaScript
  • Real-world examples: customer support bots, code reviewers, multilingual assistants, and structured data extractors
  • How to combine System Instructions with multi-turn chat
  • Best practices for writing effective instructions
  • Common errors and how to fix them
  • FAQs

This guide assumes you already have a Gemini API key and the SDK installed. If you're new to the API, start with the Gemini API Quickstart first.

What Are System Instructions?

System Instructions are pre-conversation directives sent to the model that shape its behavior throughout the entire session. Unlike regular user messages (contents), they are invisible to end users and not stored in the conversation history.

Think of System Instructions as writing the job description and rules of conduct for your AI before it starts working. They answer questions like:

  • What role does the AI play?
  • How should it speak (formal, casual, technical)?
  • What format should responses follow?
  • What topics are in or out of scope?
  • What languages should it use?

What You Can Control with System Instructions

| Control Target | Example | |---------------|---------| | Role definition | "You are a senior Python engineer at Google" | | Tone and style | "Always be concise and avoid jargon unless necessary" | | Output format | "Respond only in valid Markdown" | | Language policy | "Always respond in the user's language" | | Scope restriction | "Only answer questions about our product" | | Safety policy | "Never mention competitor products by name" |

Python Implementation

Basic Usage

Multi-turn Chat with System Instructions

System Instructions persist across all turns in a chat session — no need to re-specify them each time.

Code Reviewer Example

""", config=types.GenerateContentConfig( system_instruction=CODE_REVIEWER, ), )

print(response.text)

Expected output:

🔴 Critical Issues

SQL Injection vulnerability — never concatenate user input into SQL strings.

Fix: use parameterized queries...

Quality Score: 1/5


---

## JavaScript Implementation

```javascript
import { GoogleGenerativeAI } from "@google/generative-ai";

const genAI = new GoogleGenerativeAI(process.env.GEMINI_API_KEY);

// Initialize model with System Instructions
const model = genAI.getGenerativeModel({
  model: "gemini-2.5-pro",
  systemInstruction: {
    role: "system",
    parts: [
      {
        text: `You are a bilingual technical writer fluent in English and Japanese.
- Respond in the same language the user writes in
- Keep explanations clear and accessible to developers at any level
- Always define technical terms on first use
- Include practical examples where relevant`,
      },
    ],
  },
  generationConfig: {
    temperature: 0.7,
    maxOutputTokens: 2048,
  },
});

const chat = model.startChat();

// English question → English response
const r1 = await chat.sendMessage("What is a context window in LLMs?");
console.log(r1.response.text());

// Follow-up question — context maintained
const r2 = await chat.sendMessage("How does Gemini 2.5 Pro compare to others?");
console.log(r2.response.text());

Real-World Use Cases

1. Multilingual Customer Support Bot

2. Structured Data Extractor

Force the model to always return clean JSON — perfect for data pipelines.

Best Practices for Writing Effective System Instructions

Writing good System Instructions is part art, part engineering. Here are the principles that consistently yield the best results.

1. Define the Role Precisely

# Vague (avoid this)
"You are a helpful assistant."

# Precise (do this)
"You are a senior Python engineer with 8 years of experience building
production-grade web APIs. You favor clean, readable code over clever tricks
and always consider security implications in your suggestions."

2. Specify Output Format Explicitly

"Structure every response as follows:
1. Summary (2-3 sentences)
2. Step-by-step explanation with code examples
3. Common pitfalls to avoid
4. Related resources (if applicable)"

3. Frame Constraints Positively

# Negative framing (less effective)
"Don't talk about competitor products."

# Positive framing (more effective)
"Focus exclusively on Gemini API and Google AI Studio.
For questions about other AI services, kindly direct users
to the official documentation for those products."

4. Include Few-Shot Examples for Complex Formats

For non-standard output structures, embed examples directly in the instruction:

Example output:

Code Review

  • ✅ Simple and readable
  • ⚠️ Consider using a descriptive variable name instead of x
  • Quality Score: 4/5 """

---

## Combining with Context Caching

When your System Instructions include large reference documents (e.g., product manuals, codebases), use [Context Caching](/en/articles/gemini-api/context-caching-guide) to avoid re-sending the same tokens on every request.

```python
import os
import datetime
from google import genai
from google.genai import types

client = genai.Client(api_key=os.environ["GEMINI_API_KEY"])

with open("product_manual.txt", "r") as f:
    manual_content = f.read()

# Cache the manual content for 1 hour
cache = client.caches.create(
    model="gemini-2.5-pro",
    config=types.CreateCachedContentConfig(
        system_instruction=(
            "You are a product support specialist. "
            "Reference the product manual below to answer questions accurately."
        ),
        contents=[manual_content],
        ttl=datetime.timedelta(hours=1),
    ),
)

# Subsequent requests use the cache — no need to re-send the manual
response = client.models.generate_content(
    model="gemini-2.5-pro",
    contents="How do I perform a factory reset?",
    config=types.GenerateContentConfig(
        cached_content=cache.name,
    ),
)
print(response.text)

This approach dramatically reduces token costs and improves response latency when the same context is used across many requests.

Common Errors and Fixes

Error 1: Model Ignores System Instructions

Symptom: The model's responses don't reflect your instructions.

Fixes:

  • Make instructions more specific — vague instructions yield inconsistent results
  • Check for internal contradictions in your instruction text
  • Lower temperature to 0.3–0.5 for more deterministic behavior

Error 2: JSON Output Contains Extra Text

Fix: Use response_mime_type to enforce JSON output:

See the JSON Mode and Structured Output guide for more details.

Error 3: Instructions Stop Working Mid-Conversation

Symptom: After many turns, the model drifts from its instructions.

Fixes:

  • Very long conversations can fill the context window — periodically remind the model of key rules
  • Use Context Caching to manage context more efficiently

Error 4: 400 Bad Request

The content of your System Instructions may be triggering safety filters. Review your instructions for any content that could be interpreted as requesting harmful output.

FAQ

Q1: Do System Instructions consume tokens?

Yes. System Instructions are counted as input tokens on every request. For long instructions, using Context Caching can significantly reduce costs at scale.

Q2: Can users override System Instructions?

Not through normal conversation turns. However, prompt injection attacks ("ignore all previous instructions and...") are a real threat for user-facing apps. Always validate and sanitize user input in production environments.

Q3: Can I use multiple System Instructions?

The API accepts a single system_instruction string, but you can structure multiple roles and rules within that string using headers, numbered lists, or separator lines (---) for clarity.

Q4: Do System Instructions work with Function Calling?

Absolutely. You can instruct the model to always use a specific function when certain conditions are met: "When the user asks about weather, always call the get_weather function." See the Function Calling Guide for details.

Q5: Does this work the same way on Vertex AI?

The system_instruction parameter works identically. The only difference is the client initialization:

Conclusion

System Instructions are one of the most powerful tools in your Gemini API toolkit. By defining clear roles, output formats, and behavioral constraints upfront, you can build AI-powered applications that are consistent, predictable, and production-ready.

Key takeaways:

  • Define role, tone, format, and constraints clearly and specifically
  • Use positive framing for behavioral constraints
  • Combine with multi-turn chat to maintain context across sessions
  • Use response_mime_type="application/json" for structured data extraction
  • Leverage Context Caching when instructions include large reference materials

Ready to take your Gemini API skills further? Check out the Streaming and Multi-turn Chat Guide to learn how to build real-time, interactive experiences.

en.title: Gemini 3 thinking_level Parameter: A Complete Developer Guide
en.author: Gemini Lab
en.description: Learn how to use Gemini 3's thinking_level parameter to control reasoning depth. Optimize latency, cost, and accuracy for every use case with hands-on Python examples.
en.content:
One of the most practical additions in the Gemini 3 family is the thinking_level parameter — a developer-facing control that determines how deeply the model reasons before producing a response. By tuning this single parameter, you can strike the right balance between response speed, API cost, and answer quality depending on what your application needs.

This guide covers how thinking_level works, what each level does, when to use which, and includes working Python code you can run today.

What Is thinking_level?

Before Gemini 3 outputs a response, it runs an internal reasoning process sometimes called "thinking." This pre-output phase is where the model works through complex logic, multi-step calculations, or nuanced analysis. The thinking_level parameter lets you set how much compute the model dedicates to this phase.

Prior to Gemini 3, there was no direct way for developers to control this reasoning depth. Now you can.

Important: thinking_level is a relative guideline, not a hard token guarantee. The actual depth of reasoning adapts to the complexity of your prompt — simpler requests use fewer resources even at "high", and vice versa.

Available Levels

| Level | Description | Best For | |-------|-------------|----------| | "none" | Skips the thinking phase entirely. Fastest and cheapest. | Classification, simple Q&A | | "low" | Light reasoning only | Summarization, translation, basic inference | | "medium" | Balanced (default behavior) | General dev tasks, code generation | | "high" | Deep, thorough reasoning. Highest accuracy. | Math, logic, complex agents |

Getting Started

Install the SDK

Basic Request with thinking_level

Skip Thinking for Fast, Simple Tasks

Comparing Levels in Practice

Here's a benchmark showing how thinking_level affects latency and output quality on a multi-step reasoning task:

Choosing the Right Level by Task

| Task Type | Recommended Level | Reason | |-----------|------------------|--------| | Text classification / labeling | none | Pattern matching, no reasoning needed | | Summarization / translation | low | Lightweight context understanding | | Code generation / debugging | medium | Balanced accuracy and speed | | Math / logic problems | high | Multi-step reasoning required | | Complex agentic workflows | high | Correctness is paramount |

Using Thought Signatures for Multi-Turn Reasoning

Gemini 3 introduced Thought Signatures alongside thinking_level. A Thought Signature is an encrypted representation of the model's internal reasoning chain. By passing the signature from one turn to the next, you ensure the model maintains consistent, coherent reasoning across a long conversation — critical for agentic workflows.

Streaming with thinking_level

thinking_level works seamlessly with streaming responses:

Dynamic Level Routing to Optimize Cost

A practical pattern for production apps is to route requests to different thinking_level values based on the complexity of each task:

Common Errors and Fixes

thinking_level is not recognized

google.api_core.exceptions.InvalidArgument: 400 Unknown field: thinking_level

Cause: You're using a Gemini 2.x model, which doesn't support this parameter.

Fix: Switch to a Gemini 3 model:

Higher-than-expected latency

Cause: thinking_level="high" significantly increases processing time.

Fix: For latency-sensitive use cases, use "medium" or lower, and consider async calls to prevent blocking:

FAQ

Q: Does setting thinking_level="none" significantly degrade accuracy?

A: It depends on the task. For classification, translation, and summarization, "none" typically delivers accuracy on par with higher levels. For multi-step math or complex logical reasoning, accuracy drops noticeably — use "high" for those.

Q: Can I use thinking_level with Gemini 3 Flash?

A: Yes, gemini-3-flash-preview supports the parameter. However, Flash is architected for speed and efficiency, so even at "high", it won't match the reasoning depth of Gemini 3 Pro. Choose your model and level together based on your accuracy vs. cost requirements.

Q: Can I combine thinking_level and temperature?

A: Absolutely. They control different things: thinking_level governs reasoning depth (pre-output thinking), while temperature governs output randomness. For precision-critical tasks, thinking_level="high" + temperature=0.1 is a strong combination.

Q: Does thinking_level affect billing?

A: There's no separate line item for thinking_level, but higher levels produce more internal reasoning tokens, which count toward your usage. Routing simpler tasks to "none" or "low" can meaningfully reduce costs at scale.

Q: Does thinking_level work with async generation?

A: Yes. Pass generation_config with thinking_level to generate_content_async() the same way you would for synchronous calls. Async is especially recommended at "high" to avoid blocking your application.

Wrapping Up

thinking_level is a small parameter with a big impact. Use it strategically:

  • Speed and cost matter → "none" or "low" for simple tasks
  • Everyday dev work → "medium" as a solid default
  • Accuracy is non-negotiable → "high" for complex reasoning and agentic pipelines

Pair it with dynamic routing in production and you'll get the best of both worlds: low latency where you need it and deep reasoning where it counts. For more on Gemini 3's new capabilities, check out the Gemini 3.1 Pro guide and Function Calling walkthrough.

en.title: Building Multimodal RAG Systems with Gemini: Processing Images, Video, and Text Together
en.author: Gemini Lab
en.description: Master multimodal retrieval-augmented generation with Gemini. Learn to process images, video frames, and text in unified RAG pipelines with production patterns.
en.content:
Building Multimodal RAG Systems with Gemini: Processing Images, Video, and Text Together

Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) has transformed how we build knowledge-driven AI applications. But traditional RAG systems operate primarily in text space, missing the rich semantic information embedded in images and video. Gemini's multimodal capabilities unlock a new frontier: RAG systems that seamlessly integrate visual and textual knowledge.

This guide walks you through building production-grade multimodal RAG systems with Gemini, from architecture decisions to deployment strategies.

The Multimodal RAG Paradigm Shift

Traditional RAG systems follow a proven pattern:

  1. Chunk documents into text passages
  2. Encode text into embeddings
  3. Store embeddings in vector database
  4. Retrieve relevant passages on query
  5. Generate answer using retrieved context

Multimodal RAG extends this paradigm across modalities. A financial report might contain both explanatory text AND charts that together tell the complete story. A medical guide might pair images of symptoms with diagnostic text. Video tutorials contain both visual demonstrations and verbal explanations.

💡
Multimodal RAG is most valuable when your domain inherently combines modalities. Document processing (PDFs with charts), e-commerce (products with descriptions), and educational content benefit significantly. For purely text-based domains, the overhead may not justify the gains.

Architecture: The Unified Embedding Space

The key architectural challenge: how do you embed images, video frames, and text into a space where similarity is meaningful across modalities?

Approach 1: Shared Embedding Model

Use Gemini's unified understanding to embed diverse modalities into the same space:

This approach generates semantic summaries that capture cross-modal context. The summary becomes your embedding representation—semantically rich and meaningful for retrieval.

Approach 2: Dual Storage with Cross-Modal Retrieval

For more sophisticated systems, maintain separate storage for each modality but enable cross-modal retrieval:

Generation: Context-Aware Synthesis

With multimodal context retrieved, the generation phase becomes more nuanced. You're synthesizing from visual and textual sources simultaneously.

⚠️
When referencing images or video in generated responses, be explicit about the source. Say "As shown in the accompanying chart..." rather than vaguely referencing content. This maintains clarity and trust.

Video Processing: Temporal Extraction

Video is the most complex modality. Rather than processing the entire video at once (inefficient and error-prone), extract semantically meaningful keyframes.

Search Strategies: Multimodal Queries

Traditional RAG assumes text queries. Multimodal RAG unlocks new search patterns:

Production Considerations

Caching and Cost Optimization

Multimodal processing is expensive. Cache aggressively:

Monitoring and Quality Metrics

Track multimodal-specific metrics:

Conclusion

Multimodal RAG systems unlock knowledge from your visual and video content, enabling richer, more grounded AI responses. Start with the unified embedding approach for simpler use cases, then graduate to dual storage with cross-modal retrieval as your system scales.

The key to success is treating each modality thoughtfully—not forcing video into a text-only framework, but designing specifically for the strengths of images, video, and text when they work together.

en.title: Complete Guide to Gemini 3's thinking_level Parameter: Control Reasoning Depth
en.author: Gemini Lab
en.description: A deep dive into the thinking_level parameter introduced in Gemini 3. Learn the difference between low, medium, and high settings, how to integrate Thought Signatures, and real-world code examples to optimize cost and latency.
en.content:
Complete Guide to Gemini 3's thinking_level Parameter: Control Reasoning Depth

With the release of Gemini 3 in early 2026, Google introduced a powerful new API parameter — thinking_level — that lets developers directly control how deeply the model "thinks" before generating a response. This guide covers everything you need to know: what it is, how it works, when to use each setting, and how to implement it with real Python code examples.

What Is thinking_level?

Before generating a response, Gemini 3 internally analyzes problems through a multi-step reasoning process. The thinking_level parameter controls the maximum depth of this internal thinking process.

Importantly, Gemini 3 treats these levels as relative guidelines for reasoning depth — not as strict token guarantees. The actual amount of reasoning varies based on the complexity of the problem at hand.

Available Values

| Value | Reasoning Depth | Primary Use Case | |---|---|---| | "low" | Shallow (fast) | Simple queries, latency-sensitive apps | | "medium" | Moderate (balanced) | General tasks — recommended default | | "high" | Deep (high accuracy) | Complex reasoning, math, coding |

Why Does thinking_level Matter?

The Cost-Latency Trade-off

Internal reasoning is powerful, but it increases token consumption and response latency. By tuning thinking_level appropriately, you can:

  • Reduce costs: Use "low" for simple tasks to avoid burning unnecessary tokens
  • Improve speed: "low" skips complex reasoning chains for faster responses
  • Maximize accuracy: Use "high" for hard problems to unlock the model's full potential

PhD-Level Reasoning Performance

Even Gemini 3 Flash, when configured with thinking_level: "high", achieves state-of-the-art scores on PhD-level reasoning benchmarks:

  • GPQA Diamond: 90.4%
  • Humanity's Last Exam: 33.7% (without tools)

These results rival much larger frontier models, making Gemini 3 Flash an extremely cost-effective choice for demanding tasks.

Code Example: Basic Usage of thinking_level

The following Python example demonstrates how to call the Gemini 3 API and dynamically switch thinking_level based on task complexity.

Integrating with Thought Signatures

Alongside thinking_level, Gemini 3 introduced Thought Signatures — encrypted representations of the model's internal reasoning chain. By passing these signatures back in subsequent API calls, Gemini 3 maintains continuity of reasoning across a multi-turn conversation.

This combination is especially powerful for complex, multi-step agentic workflows.

Key Notes on Thought Signatures

  • Thought Signatures are only valid within the same model session
  • They cannot be transferred across different model versions
  • Supported only in Gemini 3 and later (not available in Gemini 2.x)

Recommended thinking_level by Use Case

Use "low" when:

  • Handling casual chitchat in a customer service chatbot
  • Running simple translation or summarization tasks
  • Streaming real-time responses where sub-second latency is required
  • Building mobile apps with strict response time requirements

Use "medium" (default recommendation) when:

  • Creating general business documents or reports
  • Performing light code reviews or bug triage
  • Generating FAQ responses or knowledge base answers
  • Writing marketing or social media content

Use "high" when:

  • Solving mathematical proofs or complex calculations
  • Designing or implementing sophisticated algorithms
  • Performing expert-level analysis in legal, medical, or scientific domains
  • Running multi-step agentic tasks requiring deep reasoning
  • Integrating with Gemini Deep Research API

Cost Comparison by Level

thinking_level directly affects token consumption. The table below shows approximate relative values (actual usage varies by prompt and problem complexity):

| Level | Relative Token Usage | Relative Latency | |---|---|---| | "low" | ×1 (baseline) | ×1 (baseline) | | "medium" | ×2–3 | ×2–3 | | "high" | ×5–10 | ×5–10 |

Cost optimization tip: Pre-classify tasks by complexity and dynamically select the appropriate level.

FAQ

Q1. What happens if I don't set thinking_level?

If you omit thinking_level, Gemini 3 will internally assess the task complexity and automatically select an appropriate reasoning depth. However, explicitly setting the parameter is recommended when you need predictable cost or latency characteristics.

Q2. Is thinking_level supported in Gemini 2.x?

No. The thinking_level parameter is only supported in Gemini 3 and later models. Passing it to Gemini 2.x will either be silently ignored or trigger an API error. Use Gemini 3 Flash or Gemini 3 Pro to access this feature.

Q3. Does thinking_level="high" guarantee accurate answers?

"high" maximizes reasoning depth but does not guarantee 100% accuracy. For tasks requiring up-to-date information, combine it with grounding_with_google_search. For precise calculations, integrate a code interpreter tool. Always validate critical outputs programmatically.

Conclusion

The thinking_level parameter is one of the most impactful additions to the Gemini 3 API. Used effectively, it enables:

  • Cost efficiency: Save on tokens by choosing the right level for each task
  • Speed and accuracy balance: From ultra-fast responses to PhD-level reasoning
  • Stronger agentic workflows: Combined with Thought Signatures for coherent multi-step tasks

Start incorporating thinking_level into your Gemini 3 projects today and unlock the full potential of the model while keeping your costs in check.

en.title: Fine-Tuning Gemini: Building Specialized Models for Domain-Specific Applications
en.author: Gemini Lab
en.description: Complete guide to fine-tuning Gemini models. Learn dataset preparation, training strategies, evaluation metrics, and production deployment for custom AI.
en.content:
Fine-Tuning Gemini: Building Specialized Models for Domain-Specific Applications

Off-the-shelf language models excel at general tasks. But when you need a model that understands your domain's language patterns, industry jargon, or specialized reasoning, fine-tuning becomes essential. Gemini's fine-tuning API enables you to build models that perform like experts in your field.

This guide covers the complete journey: from preparing datasets to evaluating models to deploying them in production. We'll focus on practical patterns that actually work.

Understanding Fine-Tuning: When and Why

Fine-tuning adjusts a pre-trained model's weights using your data. This is different from prompt engineering or RAG, which work with the model as-is. Fine-tuning permanently changes the model's behavior.

💡
Fine-tuning is most effective when: - You have domain-specific vocabulary (legal documents, medical notes, financial reports) - You need consistent output formatting (structured data extraction, specific JSON schemas) - Your domain has unique reasoning patterns (specialized diagnostic procedures) - You're willing to invest in quality data preparation (at least 100-200 high-quality examples)

It's less effective for tasks better solved by RAG, few-shot prompting, or basic tool integration.

Fine-Tuning vs. Alternatives

For compliance extraction where you process thousands of documents and need consistent, specialized extraction, fine-tuning pays for itself.

Dataset Preparation: The Foundation

Fine-tuning quality depends entirely on your data. Garbage in, garbage out applies here more than anywhere else.

Data Format and Structure

Gemini expects training data in a specific format. Each training example should include:

⚠️
Data quality directly impacts model quality. Spend time: - Removing duplicates - Fixing inconsistent formatting - Validating output correctness - Ensuring examples are representative - Checking for biases or artifacts

A smaller, cleaner dataset often beats a larger, noisier one.

Training Configuration and Hyperparameters

Once your data is ready, configure the training job. Gemini exposes key hyperparameters:

Hyperparameter Tuning Strategy

Evaluation: Measuring Model Quality

Before deploying, rigorously evaluate your fine-tuned model. Generic metrics (loss, accuracy) matter less than domain-specific metrics.

💡
Choose evaluation metrics that match your business goals. For legal document analysis, you might care about: - Precision of clause extraction (false positives are costly) - Recall of risk indicators (missing risks is dangerous) - Consistency across document types - Human reviewer alignment

Production Deployment

Once satisfied with evaluation results, deploy the model with proper versioning and monitoring.

Real-World Example: Financial Analysis Model

Here's a complete example of fine-tuning a model for financial document analysis:

Conclusion

Fine-tuning Gemini transforms a general-purpose model into a specialist for your domain. The key to success is investing in quality data, carefully choosing evaluation metrics that match your business goals, and thoroughly testing before production.

Start small—fine-tune on a focused task with high-quality data. Once you see consistent improvements, expand to related tasks. And always maintain a fallback strategy to the base model as you shift traffic to your custom versions.

en.title: Gemini File API Complete Guide: Uploading and Using Files
en.author: Gemini Lab
en.description: A comprehensive guide to uploading, managing, and using files with the Gemini File API. Includes practical Python code for images, PDFs, videos, and audio.
en.content:
Introduction

The Gemini API includes a File API that lets you upload files separately from your prompt. This is especially useful when working with large files or when you want to reuse media across multiple requests.

In this guide, we'll walk through the Gemini File API from the basics to practical use cases, complete with Python code examples.

Prerequisites

  • Python 3.9 or higher
  • A Gemini API key (available from Google AI Studio)
  • The google-genai package installed

What Is the File API?

The File API is a media file management service built into the Gemini API. Its key characteristics include:

  • Free to use (no additional cost for the File API itself)
  • 48-hour storage: uploaded files are retained for 48 hours
  • Max file size: up to 2 GB per file (use the File API when the total request exceeds 100 MB)
  • Supported formats: images, PDFs, videos, audio, text, and more

When Should You Use the File API?

If the total request size (files + text prompt + system instructions) exceeds 100 MB, using the File API is required. Below that threshold you can pass files inline as Base64-encoded data, but the File API improves reusability across requests.

Basic Usage

1. Initialize the Client

2. Upload a File

On success, myfile.uri contains the unique URI for your file.

3. Generate Content Using the Uploaded File

Working with PDFs

PDFs are one of the most common use cases. You can process PDFs up to 50 MB in size.

Working with Videos

Video processing takes time, so you need to wait until the file enters the ACTIVE state before using it.

Combining Multiple Files

You can also combine multiple files with text in a single request.

Managing Files

List All Uploaded Files

Get File Details

Delete a File

Frequently Asked Questions

Q: Is the File API free? A: Yes, uploading and managing files with the File API is free. However, standard Gemini API pricing applies when you use uploaded files in content generation requests.

Q: How long are files stored? A: Files are automatically deleted 48 hours after upload. You can delete them earlier using client.files.delete().

Q: What file formats are supported? A: Images (JPEG, PNG, WebP, HEIC, HEIF), video (MP4, MOV, AVI, etc.), audio (MP3, WAV, FLAC, etc.), PDF, and text formats (TXT, HTML, CSS, Markdown, etc.).

Q: Can I use a file's URI from a different project? A: No. File URIs are tied to the project associated with the API key used for the upload. Keep your API key secure to prevent unauthorized access to your uploaded files.

Summary

The Gemini File API makes working with large files and reusing media across multiple requests much more efficient. Key takeaways:

  • The File API is free; files are retained for 48 hours
  • Required for requests exceeding 100 MB total
  • Supports a wide range of file formats: images, PDFs, video, and audio
  • Videos must finish processing before they can be used
  • File URIs are tied to your API key's project — keep keys secure

As next steps, check out the Gemini API Multimodal Guide and the Context Caching Guide.

en.title: Gemini 3.1 Flash Lite Complete Guide — Fastest, Most Cost-Efficient Model
en.author: Gemini Lab
en.description: Gemini 3.1 Flash Lite is Google's most cost-efficient AI model. Learn about its features, $0.25/1M token pricing, 2.5x faster response times, and how to integrate it into your apps.
en.content:
Gemini 3.1 Flash Lite Complete Guide — Fastest, Most Cost-Efficient Model

Introduction

In 2026, Google introduced Gemini 3.1 Flash Lite — the most cost-efficient model in the Gemini family. It combines blazing-fast response times with ultra-low pricing, making it ideal for high-volume production applications. This guide covers everything you need to know: features, pricing, and practical implementation examples.

What Is Gemini 3.1 Flash Lite?

Gemini 3.1 Flash Lite is Google's lightest-weight model, positioned as the most cost-effective option in the Gemini lineup. It's available as a developer preview through the Gemini API on Google AI Studio, and also offered via Vertex AI for enterprise use cases.

Key Features

  • Ultra-low cost: $0.25/1M input tokens, $1.50/1M output tokens — among the most affordable in the industry
  • Super-fast response: Time to first token (TTFT) is 2.5x faster than Gemini 2.5 Flash
  • High throughput: Output speed is 45% higher compared to Gemini 2.5 Flash
  • Multimodal support: Handles text, images, and video inputs

Model Comparison

| Model | Best For | Input Price | Output Price | Speed | |-------|----------|-------------|--------------|-------| | Gemini 3.1 Flash Lite | Speed & cost | $0.25/1M | $1.50/1M | ★★★★★ | | Gemini 2.5 Flash | Balanced | $0.075/1M | $0.30/1M | ★★★★ | | Gemini 3.1 Pro | High accuracy | Higher | Higher | ★★★ |

ℹ️
Gemini 3.1 Flash Lite is ideal for production environments handling large request volumes and applications where real-time responsiveness is critical.

Best Use Cases

Where It Shines

  1. Chatbots & Customer Support: Handle high volumes of user requests at minimal cost
  2. Real-time Translation: Low-latency translation for live communication apps
  3. Content Classification & Tagging: Automated categorization of large text datasets
  4. Summarization: Fast summarization of news, articles, and documents
  5. Mobile App Integration: AI features where low latency is non-negotiable

Where to Use a Different Model

  • Complex reasoning or deep analytical tasks (→ consider Gemini 3.1 Pro)
  • High-accuracy code generation (→ Gemini 3.1 Pro is better suited)

Implementation

Python

Streaming

TypeScript

Image Analysis (Multimodal)

Using Google AI Studio

You can experiment without writing any code directly in your browser:

  1. Go to aistudio.google.com
  2. Select "Gemini 3.1 Flash Lite" from the model dropdown
  3. Enter your prompt and click "Run"
💡
Google AI Studio lets you try Gemini 3.1 Flash Lite within the free tier. It's a great way to prototype ideas quickly before moving to a production API integration.

Cost Optimization Tips

1. Route Tasks to the Right Model

Not every request needs the most powerful model. Routing tasks based on complexity can dramatically cut costs:

2. Optimize Your Prompts

Concise, clear prompts reduce token count and lower costs:

3. Use Batch Processing

FAQ

Q: Is Gemini 3.1 Flash Lite better than Gemini 2.5 Flash?

A: It depends on your needs. Flash Lite wins on speed and cost, making it ideal for high-volume, latency-sensitive workloads. If you need a balance of capability and cost, Gemini 2.5 Flash is still a great choice.

Q: Is there a free tier?

A: Yes, Google AI Studio's free plan includes a certain number of requests per day. For large-scale production use, you'll need a paid plan.

Q: Can I use it with Vertex AI?

A: Yes, Gemini 3.1 Flash Lite is available on Vertex AI for enterprise customers who need higher security, SLAs, or compliance requirements.

Q: What's the context window size?

A: Please refer to the official Google documentation for the latest specs. Like other Gemini models, it supports a large context window suitable for most use cases.

Summary

Gemini 3.1 Flash Lite is the go-to model when speed and cost efficiency are top priorities. Here are the key takeaways:

  • Ultra-low pricing: $0.25/1M input tokens — industry-leading cost efficiency
  • Blazing fast: TTFT is 2.5x faster than Gemini 2.5 Flash
  • Versatile: Multimodal support for text and images
  • Easy migration: Just swap the model name in your existing Gemini API code

Whether you're building a production chatbot, a real-time translation service, or a content moderation pipeline, Gemini 3.1 Flash Lite delivers the performance you need at a fraction of the cost. For more details, check out the official Google AI documentation.

en.title: Gemini Embeddings API Guide — Semantic Search, Similarity, and RAG
en.author: Gemini Lab
en.description: Build semantic search, document similarity, and RAG (Retrieval Augmented Generation) using the Gemini Embeddings API.
en.content:
What Are Embeddings?

Embeddings convert text into numerical vectors. Text with similar meaning ends up close together in vector space, enabling "semantic similarity" searches that keyword matching can't handle.

The Gemini Embeddings API converts text to high-quality vectors for use in your applications.

Use Cases

  • Semantic search: Knowledge bases that search by meaning
  • Document similarity: Duplicate detection, related content recommendations
  • RAG (Retrieval Augmented Generation): Search docs first, then pass context to an LLM
  • Text classification: Classification with minimal training data
  • Clustering: Grouping large volumes of feedback or comments

Basic Usage

task_type Options

Setting the right task_type improves embedding quality:

| task_type | Use case | |-----------|----------| | retrieval_query | Search queries / questions | | retrieval_document | Documents to be searched | | semantic_similarity | Sentence similarity scoring | | classification | Text classification | | clustering | Text grouping |

Semantic Search with Cosine Similarity

Simple RAG Implementation

Summary

The Gemini Embeddings API makes it straightforward to build semantically-aware applications.

  • text-embedding-004 delivers high-quality 768-dimension vectors
  • Setting task_type correctly improves accuracy
  • Cosine similarity enables fast nearest-neighbor search
  • RAG patterns augment LLM knowledge with up-to-date retrieved content

Pair it with a vector database (Pinecone, Weaviate, pgvector) for production-scale search systems.

en.title: Gemini API Context Caching Guide — Cut Large Processing Costs by 90%
en.author: Gemini Lab
en.description: Use Gemini API's Context Caching to drastically reduce costs when repeatedly processing the same documents or large prompts.
en.content:
What Is Context Caching?

Gemini API's Context Caching lets you upload a large prompt or document once, cache it, and reuse that cache in subsequent requests — without paying to re-process it every time.

This is especially useful when asking multiple questions about the same material, or for agents that repeatedly reference a large codebase.

Why It Cuts Costs

In a standard Gemini API request, you're billed for every input token every time. With a 100,000-token document and 10 questions:

  • Without cache: 100,000 × 10 = 1M tokens in input costs
  • With cache: 100,000 (first time) + 10 × (small questions only) = major savings

Cached tokens are billed at a lower rate than regular input tokens, so the effective cost savings can exceed 90% for repeated-access workloads.

Setup

1. Create a cache

2. Use the cache for queries

3. List and delete caches

Best Use Cases

Context caching is most effective for:

Document QA: Chatbots that answer repeated questions about long PDFs or specs

Codebase analysis: Agents that load a large repository and run multiple analyses

Legal/medical documents: Analyzing long contracts or papers from multiple angles

Data processing pipelines: Processing many records using the same large prompt template

Cost Example

Using Gemini 3 Flash with a 500,000-token document accessed 50 times:

| Method | Total input tokens | Estimated cost | |--------|-------------------|----------------| | No cache | 25,000,000 | $12.50 | | With cache | 500,000 + small amounts | ~$1.00 |

The larger the document relative to the questions, the bigger the savings.

TTL Configuration

Caches require a TTL (Time-To-Live). The range is 1 minute to 1 hour.

  • Short tasks: 5–10 minutes is sufficient
  • Long-running processing: Use the full 1-hour max
  • Regular reuse: Schedule a script to recreate the cache as needed

Caches are auto-deleted after TTL expires. You can also extend the expiry via the API.

Summary

Context Caching is one of the most impactful ways to reduce Gemini API costs for repeated-access workflows.

  • Dramatically cuts costs when the same document is accessed multiple times
  • Simple to implement and easy to add to existing code
  • Most effective for long-document Q&A, agents, and batch processing

If you're running large-scale or repeated-document workloads, Context Caching is worth adding.

en.title: Gemini 3 Pro Complete Guide — When to Use Google's Top-Tier Model
en.author: Gemini Lab
en.description: Everything about Gemini 3 Pro: features, benchmarks, pricing, and when to use it versus Gemini 3 Flash. A practical guide to Google's flagship model.
en.content:
What Is Gemini 3 Pro?

Gemini 3 Pro is Google's highest-tier AI model released in 2026. While Gemini 3 Flash prioritizes cost and speed, 3 Pro is built for maximum reasoning quality.

It's the right tool for scientific research, medical analysis, complex code architecture, and tasks where precision matters more than throughput.

Key Features

Advanced Reasoning

The standout feature of Gemini 3 Pro is its ability to accurately solve complex multi-step problems — advanced mathematics, multi-hop logic, code architecture, and professional-grade analysis.

1M Token Context Window

Process entire books, thousands of pages of technical documentation, or massive codebases in a single request.

Multimodal Processing

Handles text, images, audio, video, and PDFs simultaneously — ideal for complex multimedia analysis tasks.

Deep Think Mode

Enable extended reasoning for the hardest problems: mathematical proofs, complex algorithm design, and multi-step logical reasoning.

Benchmark Comparison

| Benchmark | Gemini 3 Pro | Gemini 3 Flash | What it measures | |-----------|-------------|----------------|------------------| | ARC-AGI-2 | 87.5% | 75%+ | General reasoning | | GPQA Diamond | 92% | 90.4% | PhD-level science | | MATH | 97% | 95%+ | Math problems | | SWE-bench | 80% | 78% | Coding tasks |

Pro leads across benchmarks, but the gap has narrowed significantly with Gemini 3 Flash. The cost-performance tradeoff is the main deciding factor.

Pricing

| Type | Rate | |------|------| | Input tokens | $1.25 / 1M tokens | | Output tokens | $10.00 / 1M tokens |

Compared to Gemini 3 Flash ($0.50/$3.00): 2.5× input cost, 3.3× output cost. Reserve Pro for tasks where quality justifies the premium.

Usage

Python API

Enable Deep Think Mode

Gemini 3 Pro vs. Gemini 3 Flash

Choose Gemini 3 Pro when:

  • Medical, legal, or scientific analysis requires maximum accuracy
  • Complex code architecture or refactoring decisions
  • Nuanced analysis of long contracts or research papers
  • Mathematical proofs or rigorous logical problems
  • Output quality is the priority and cost is secondary

Choose Gemini 3 Flash when:

  • Processing large volumes of requests
  • Real-time chat applications
  • General coding assistance or document generation
  • Cost efficiency matters in production

For most development tasks, Gemini 3 Flash delivers sufficient quality. Reserve Pro for cases where "best possible quality" is non-negotiable.

Summary

Gemini 3 Pro is Google's highest-quality model as of 2026 — built for tasks requiring maximum reasoning precision.

  • Best for complex reasoning, high-accuracy analysis
  • 1M token context handles even massive documents
  • Costs ~3× more than Gemini 3 Flash — use selectively
  • Deep Think mode boosts accuracy on the hardest problems

The rule of thumb: Gemini 3 Flash for development and production scale, Gemini 3 Pro for tasks where quality is worth the premium.

en.title: Gemini 3 Flash Complete Guide — Frontier AI at Flash-Level Speed and Lower Cost
en.author: Gemini Lab
en.description: A complete guide to Google's Gemini 3 Flash: features, benchmarks, pricing, and how to get started. Covers 1M context, multimodal input, and configurable reasoning.
en.content:
Introduction

In March 2026, Google officially released Gemini 3 Flash — a next-generation AI model designed to deliver frontier intelligence at Flash-level speed and cost. This model significantly outperforms the 2.5 series while keeping pricing accessible for developers and enterprises alike.

This guide covers Gemini 3 Flash's capabilities, benchmarks, pricing, and practical usage examples for developers.

What Is Gemini 3 Flash?

Gemini 3 Flash is Google's latest large language model that redefines what "Flash" means in the Gemini lineup. Rather than trading off performance for speed, Gemini 3 Flash delivers Pro-grade reasoning at Flash-level latency and cost.

Key capabilities include:

  • 1 million token context window: Process massive codebases, documents, and conversations in a single call
  • Multimodal input support: Text, images, audio, video, and PDFs
  • Configurable reasoning levels: Choose from minimal, low, medium, or high thinking depth
  • Automatic context caching: Reduce costs on repeated processing
  • Structured output and tool use: Built for production agentic applications

Benchmark Performance

Gemini 3 Flash sets a new bar for performance in its class, surpassing many frontier models including its Pro counterpart.

| Benchmark | Score | Description | |-----------|-------|-------------| | GPQA Diamond | 90.4% | PhD-level reasoning and knowledge | | Humanity's Last Exam | 33.7% | Hardest exam without tools | | SWE-bench Verified | 78% | Agentic software engineering |

Notably, its SWE-bench Verified score of 78% surpasses Gemini 3 Pro on coding tasks, making it the top choice for software development use cases.

Pricing

Gemini 3 Flash is available through the Gemini API and Vertex AI at the following rates:

| Type | Price | |------|-------| | Input tokens | $0.50 / 1M tokens | | Output tokens | $3.00 / 1M tokens |

Compared to Gemini 2.5 Pro ($1.25/1M input), Gemini 3 Flash cuts input costs by more than half, making it an economical choice for high-volume applications.

How to Use Gemini 3 Flash

Try It in Google AI Studio

The quickest way to get started is Google AI Studio. Select gemini-3-flash-preview from the model selector and start prompting — no API key required for testing.

Gemini API (Python)

Setting Reasoning Levels

Gemini 3 Flash lets you control thinking depth across four levels. Use higher reasoning for complex tasks and lower for latency-sensitive applications.

Multimodal Input (Images)

Gemini CLI Integration

Gemini 3 Flash is also available in the Gemini CLI for terminal-based workflows.

Best Use Cases

Here are the scenarios where Gemini 3 Flash truly shines.

Code generation and review: With a 78% SWE-bench score, it excels at bug fixing, refactoring, and code review — often outperforming dedicated coding models.

Document analysis at scale: The 1M token context makes it ideal for processing large PDFs, financial reports, or legal documents in a single request.

Multimodal applications: Build real-time apps that combine text, images, and audio at low cost and high speed.

Agentic development: With built-in tool use and function calling, it integrates naturally into Google Antigravity and other agentic platforms.

Games and interactive experiences: Low latency and high reasoning make it suitable for game AI and interactive characters.

Gemini 3 Flash vs. Gemini 2.5 Pro

| Feature | Gemini 3 Flash | Gemini 2.5 Pro | |---------|---------------|----------------| | Input cost | $0.50/1M | $1.25/1M | | SWE-bench | 78% | ~63% | | GPQA Diamond | 90.4% | ~84% | | Speed | ◎ Fast | ○ Moderate | | Reasoning levels | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | | Context window | 1M | 1M |

For most new projects, Gemini 3 Flash is the better starting point given its superior performance at a lower price point.

FAQ

Q: Is Gemini 3 Flash publicly available? A: As of March 2026, it is available to developers through the Gemini API and Vertex AI, and to general users through the Gemini app.

Q: Is there a free tier? A: Google AI Studio offers a free tier with usage limits. Check the official AI Studio documentation for the latest quota information.

Q: Can I migrate from Gemini 2.5 Flash with minimal changes? A: Yes. Simply change the model name to gemini-3-flash-preview — the API interface remains the same.

Q: What's the difference between Gemini 3 Flash and Gemini 3.1 Flash Lite? A: Gemini 3.1 Flash Lite is a lighter, faster, and more cost-efficient variant optimized for simple tasks. Use 3.1 Flash Lite for lightweight processing and 3 Flash when higher performance is needed.

Summary

Gemini 3 Flash is Google's latest model released in March 2026, delivering frontier-level intelligence at Flash speed and cost.

  • SWE-bench 78% and GPQA Diamond 90.4% set new performance benchmarks
  • $0.50/1M input token pricing offers strong cost efficiency
  • Four configurable reasoning levels for flexible task handling
  • Full multimodal support with a 1M token context window

If you're starting a new AI project, Gemini 3 Flash is the model to try first. Get started for free in Google AI Studio today.

en.title: Gemini Live API Complete Guide: Building Real-Time Voice and Video Conversations
en.author: Gemini Lab
en.description: A practical guide to building real-time voice and video conversational AI with the Gemini Live API on Vertex AI, from setup to full implementation examples.
en.content:
Introduction

In early 2026, Google officially released the Gemini Live API as Generally Available (GA) on Vertex AI. This API enables developers to build real-time conversational AI that combines voice, video, and text in a single, low-latency stream. From call centers to educational apps and interactive assistants, the use cases are vast and compelling.

In this guide, we'll walk through everything you need to know — from what the Gemini Live API is, to step-by-step implementation with working code examples.

What Is the Gemini Live API?

The Gemini Live API is designed for low-latency, multimodal real-time conversation. Unlike standard chat APIs, it offers:

  • Bidirectional real-time communication: WebSocket-based streaming for audio and video data
  • Simultaneous audio and video processing: Handle microphone and camera input at the same time
  • Low latency: Fast response times to maintain natural conversation flow
  • Gemini 2.5 Flash Native Audio model: Native audio understanding and synthesis built in

With GA availability on Vertex AI, you can combine the Live API with enterprise-grade SLAs and the full security ecosystem of Google Cloud Platform (GCP).

Prerequisites

Before diving in, it helps to be familiar with:

  • Basic Python programming
  • Google Cloud Platform (GCP) fundamentals
  • A conceptual understanding of WebSockets (deep knowledge not required)
  • Gemini API basics (see Gemini API Quickstart)

Setup

1. Prepare Your Google Cloud Project

First, enable the Vertex AI API in your GCP project.

2. Install the Gen AI SDK

As of 2026, the Gemini Live API requires the Google Gen AI SDK (the Vertex AI SDK will drop Gemini support after June 2026, so migrating to the Gen AI SDK is strongly recommended).

3. Set Environment Variables

Basic Implementation

Text-Based Real-Time Conversation

Let's start with the simplest case: bidirectional text conversation.

Voice Input and Output

Here's how to implement microphone input and speaker output for a spoken conversation.

Advanced Use: Adding Video Input

You can also process live video from a camera alongside audio.

Customizing with System Instructions

You can fine-tune the AI's behavior using system_instruction in your config.

FAQ

Q: How is the Gemini Live API priced?

A: Pricing on Vertex AI is based on the number of tokens processed for audio input and output. Billing officially began on January 28, 2026. See the Vertex AI Pricing page for current rates.

Q: What languages are supported?

A: The Live API supports a wide range of languages, including robust support for English and Japanese in both speech recognition and speech synthesis.

Q: Is there a maximum session duration?

A: Yes, the maximum session duration per connection is 15 minutes. For longer interactions, implement a reconnection strategy in your application.

Q: Can I still use the Vertex AI SDK?

A: Vertex AI SDK releases after June 2026 will no longer support Gemini. New Gemini features are only available in the Google Gen AI SDK. Migrating is straightforward — the main change is switching from the google-cloud-aiplatform package to google-genai.

Q: What kind of latency can I expect?

A: In general, you can expect 100ms–300ms of latency. This can vary based on your network conditions and the GCP region you're connecting to.

Summary

The Gemini Live API dramatically simplifies building real-time multimodal conversational AI that combines voice, video, and text. Its GA availability on Vertex AI makes it ready for production-grade, enterprise-scale deployments.

Key takeaways:

  • Use the Gen AI SDK (google-genai) to access Gemini Live API on Vertex AI
  • Supports audio-only, text-only, or combined audio + video configurations
  • Customize AI behavior with system_instruction for domain-specific applications
  • Billing started January 28, 2026 — plan your pricing model accordingly

As a next step, consider building out real-world use cases such as call center automation, accessibility tools, or interactive educational tutors.

en.title: Build Web Apps with Gemini 2.5 Pro — Testing the WebDev Arena #1
en.author: Gemini Lab
en.description: Gemini 2.5 Pro ranked #1 on WebDev Arena. Learn how to leverage its web app generation capabilities with practical prompting tips and development workflows.
en.content:
Building Web Apps with the #1 Ranked AI

Gemini 2.5 Pro has claimed the top spot on the WebDev Arena leaderboard — a benchmark that measures AI models' ability to build aesthetically pleasing and functional web apps based on human preference.

This ranking reflects Gemini 2.5 Pro's exceptional quality in web app generation. This article shows you how to get the most out of it.

Development Flow from Google AI Studio

Step 1: Prototype in AI Studio

Go to Google AI Studio and select Gemini 2.5 Pro.

Build a simple Pomodoro timer web app.
Requirements:
- 25-minute work / 5-minute break countdown
- Display remaining time with a large circular progress bar
- Sound notification on session completion
- Dark theme
- Responsive design (desktop and mobile)
- Single HTML file

Gemini 2.5 Pro generates a fully working web app from this prompt alone.

Step 2: Iterate

Review the generated app and refine through chat:

Add the option to choose between 25-minute and 50-minute work sessions.
Also show today's completed Pomodoro count at the bottom of the screen.

Step 3: Deploy

Transfer your code from AI Studio to Antigravity, or deploy directly to Cloudflare Pages or Vercel.

Prompting Best Practices

Be Specific About Requirements

❌ "Build a weather app"
✅ "Build a web app that shows the weather for my current location.
   Use OpenWeather API.
   Display temperature, humidity, weather icon, and 3-day forecast.
   Use glassmorphism card design.
   Single HTML file."

Specify Design Style

Gemini 2.5 Pro responds well to design direction:

  • "Glassmorphism," "Neumorphism," "Minimalist"
  • "Apple-style," "Material Design"
  • Color palette (e.g., "Use #667EEA as the primary color")
  • Animations (e.g., "Fade in," "Slide transitions")

Build Incrementally

For larger apps, add features step by step:

  1. Start with a basic MVP
  2. Refine the UI design
  3. Add features incrementally
  4. Improve responsiveness and accessibility

Where Gemini 2.5 Pro Shines

Single-File Apps

Gemini excels at generating self-contained HTML/CSS/JavaScript apps. Tools, dashboards, calculators, games — it handles a wide range of categories.

Visual Quality

One reason it tops WebDev Arena is the beauty of its generated UIs. Expect polished designs with effective use of gradients, shadows, and animations.

Interactivity

It can generate apps with advanced interactions including drag-and-drop, keyboard shortcuts, and real-time previews.

Practical Example: Sales Dashboard

Build a sales dashboard web app.

Requirements:
- Dark theme, sidebar navigation
- KPI cards: total revenue, order count, average order value, month-over-month change
- Monthly revenue line chart (using Chart.js)
- Regional sales pie chart
- Recent orders table
- Include demo data
- Single HTML file

Takeaway

Gemini 2.5 Pro's web app generation capability is exceptional, as the benchmark results confirm. By providing specific requirements and design direction in your prompts, you can generate professional web apps in minutes. Try it for free on Google AI Studio.

en.title: Gemini API Logging & Datasets Tool — A Practical Guide
en.author: Gemini Lab
en.description: Learn how to use the new Gemini API logging and datasets tool. Record API requests, analyze usage patterns, and streamline model evaluation.
en.content:
A New Way to Monitor Your API Usage

In March 2026, Google released a new logging and datasets tool for the Gemini API. This tool lets you record, analyze, and manage API request data directly from the Google AI Studio dashboard.

Previously, developers had to build their own logging infrastructure. Now, API usage monitoring is a built-in feature.

Key Features

Automatic Request Logging

API calls are automatically logged with both request and response data:

  • Input prompts (text, images, video)
  • Output responses
  • Token usage
  • Latency
  • Model name and version
  • Error information

Dataset Creation and Management

Create datasets from logs for model evaluation or fine-tuning:

Filtering and Search

Quickly find specific requests in large volumes of logs:

  • Date range filtering
  • Model version filtering
  • Response quality score filtering
  • Text search (search within prompt content)

Use Cases

Prompt Quality Improvement

Analyze logs to quantitatively understand which prompts produce good results and which fall short of expectations.

Cost Optimization

Review token usage at the request level to identify expensive calls. Optimize through prompt trimming or model selection (Pro vs Flash).

Model Evaluation

Run the same prompt set against different model versions for easy A/B testing:

Anomaly Detection

Detect sudden changes in response quality or spikes in error rates for early problem identification.

Viewing in Google AI Studio

  1. Go to Google AI Studio
  2. Select "Logs & Datasets" from the left menu
  3. Review log summaries on the dashboard
  4. Apply filters for detailed analysis

Privacy and Security

  • Log data is scoped to your project and inaccessible to other users
  • Logs containing sensitive data are stored encrypted
  • Default retention period is 30 days (customizable)
  • Operates in GDPR and SOC2 compliant environments

Things to Note

  • The logging feature is currently in beta
  • Large log volumes may incur storage costs
  • Real-time log streaming is planned for a future update

Takeaway

The Gemini API logging and datasets tool is essential for developers running the API in production. It enables meaningful improvements across three key dimensions: prompt quality, cost optimization, and model evaluation.

en.title: Gemini 2.5 Pro TTS — The New Era of AI Voice Synthesis
en.author: Gemini Lab
en.description: Explore Gemini 2.5 Pro TTS preview — featuring enhanced expressivity, precision pacing, and seamless dialogue. Learn how to use the new TTS model in your projects.
